title: Experimental Design in Biomedical Engineering
text:
Preface







This book was developed specifically to support students attending the course Experimental Design in Biomedical Engineering at Politecnico di Torino. Its content, organization, and practical activities closely follow the structure of the course, integrating lectures with hands-on exercises designed to reinforce the concepts discussed in class. Nevertheless, I hope that students from other institutions, as well as colleagues involved in teaching or conducting biomedical research, may also find the material useful as an introduction to experimental design and statistical reasoning in the health sciences.
This book originated from two observations that have emerged over many years of teaching and supervising Biomedical Engineering students.
The first concerns the way students approach scientific research. Many are comfortable collecting data and exploring them in search of interesting patterns. Exploratory analyses undoubtedly have their place, particularly when learning a new experimental technique or becoming familiar with an unfamiliar dataset. However, exploratory analyses should not replace the scientific method.
Every scientific study should begin with a question.
A relevant scientific question motivates the entire experimental process. The Introduction establishes why that question is worth asking and presents the scientific evidence supporting the proposed hypothesis. The Methods describe how the hypothesis will be tested, requiring careful definition of the experimental design, appropriate control of confounding variables, and rigorous data collection and processing procedures. The Results present the evidence obtained, reporting the findings objectively and without interpretation. Finally, the Discussion critically examines whether the results support or reject the original hypothesis and places those findings within the context of the existing scientific literature.
Once this logical sequence is understood, experimental design becomes far more than a collection of procedures. It becomes a structured way of thinking scientifically.
The second motivation for writing this book arose from a gap I observed in the education of Biomedical Engineering students. Although concepts related to statistics are introduced throughout several courses, they are often presented independently and with different objectives. Students therefore become familiar with individual statistical techniques but frequently lack a unified understanding of inferential statistics, when different methods should be applied, and how their results should be interpreted in the context of biomedical research.
This book was written to help bridge that gap.
Rather than presenting statistics as a collection of formulas to be memorized, the book integrates experimental design, data acquisition, data processing, statistical analysis, and scientific reporting into a single coherent framework. The practical activities, based on postural control experiments and implemented using the R programming language, were designed to reinforce this integration by allowing students to experience the complete research workflow—from formulating a hypothesis to communicating scientific findings.
My hope is that this book helps its readers develop not only the technical skills required to perform statistical analyses, but also the critical thinking necessary to design meaningful experiments, interpret evidence responsibly, and communicate scientific discoveries with clarity and rigor.
Above all, I hope this book reminds its readers that every meaningful experiment begins not with data, nor with statistics, but with a well-formulated scientific question.

title: Introduction to Experimental Design
text:
Experimental Design Issues
Research, manufacturing, and clinical settings are fields where biomedical engineers (BEs) play a key role. Among the many possible contributions that a BE may provide in these fields, three are of major interest:
But how can the BE ascertain whether a given procedure is better than another? On which basis may the BE determine that a new tool is superior to existing tools? How and which variables need to be considered to assess the effect of a condition on our health status?
These issues can only be addressed through well-designed experiments
As quoted by Susan White [1]:  AI-generated illustration created with ChatGPT (OpenAI), 2026
Before presenting a definition for Experimental Design, its importance should be clarified. Some examples may help understanding its relevance.
Suppose you are asked to conduct an experiment1 to test the idea that coffee consumption may impact cardiovascular health. You decide to recruit a sample of subjects and stratify them according to their daily coffee consumption. You measure their heart rate and hypothesize that subjects who drink more coffee may exhibit a higher resting heart rate. This is exactly the result you obtain, and you may therefore be tempted to publish your study, concluding that drinking too much coffee may negatively impact health.
After some time, you receive a letter from a colleague asking: did you control for lifestyle factors (e.g. physical activity) when recruiting subjects? You reply that you did not. Your colleague then points out that your results may be of dubious validity. Can you guess why lifestyle would be a confounder2?
For interested reader, this review by Mendonza and colleagues [2] provides an insightful view about the impact of coffee consumption on health.
Another example concerns testing a new drug for weight loss. In this case, you work within a team to evaluate whether a new FDA-approved drug is effective. The team recruits a group of participants and measures body mass before, two months after, and four months after the drug is administered. Your team observes a significant3 decrease in body mass.
How confident can you be that the observed effect was due to the new drug? If the drug was tested on subjects in the Northern hemisphere, from January to May, a similar weight loss effect might also be observed in people not taking the drug (controls4): people tend to prepare themselves for the summer.

title: Introduction to Experimental Design
text:
Definition of Experimental Design
There are many definitions of Experimental Design in the literature. This variety likely stems from the different fields in which experiments are designed. In a nutshell, we may say that:

Experimental Design is the art of designing experiments

A more formal definition, applicable to the biomedical field, would be the planning of actions necessary for testing hypotheses of documented relevance in health sciences.
Key elements common to the design of experiments entail three main steps, as detailed in the following sections.

INTRODUCTION: relevance of experiments
METHODS: study design
RESULTS & DISCUSSION: communicating your discovery




Stating the Relevance of Experiments
Regardless of the field of application, any experiment should be motivated by a sound question. The relevance of such a question should be clear, and meaningful questions can only be formulated based on previous knowledge or well-established grounds.
Before designing a study, you should provide clear answers to the following questions:

What is your question?—the open problem, i.e. a gap in current knowledge
How would addressing your question contribute to advancing knowledge?—the importance of the problem
Why do you ask your question?—the necessity of your study (the contribution of your study should be substantial, not incremental)
What do you expect to observe?—your hypothesis

All these points should be covered in the Introduction section of a study.




Designing the Study
In practical terms, designing the study consists of identifying the most appropriate procedures to test a given hypothesis. When planning procedures, you must explicitly define each of the following points:

Independent variable: what will you manipulate to test your hypothesis?
Dependent variable: what will you measure?
Confounding variables: is it necessary to control for other variables that may influence the outcome?
Define the study design: will any intervention be applied?
Experimental units: how many participants (units) are needed to ensure reliable results?
Data collection: what will you use, and how, to measure the variables of interest?
Data analysis: how will the data be processed to obtain metrics of interest5?

All these points should be defined before starting data collection. Only in this way is it possible to ensure that experiments are conceived on solid grounds rather than on convenience.






NoteWhat is this book about?



The course Experimental Design in Biomedical Engineering covers points 4 and 5 to a limited extent, and point 7 extensively. Point 7 is mainly focused on understanding and applying statistical methods.




While most BEs successfully reach the point of computing the metrics of interest, many stop there. The final step consists of using statistics6 to make a confident statement about the study hypothesis: do your results support your hypothesis or not?
We encounter statistics frequently—when watching a soccer match or checking the distribution of students’ grades. Although statistical methods are defined in the METHODS phase, their real value emerges when reporting findings.




Reporting Findings
You may have conducted the most relevant experiment in the world, yielding highly impactful results. However, this effort would be wasted without a clear and accurate report communicating your findings.
After collecting and analyzing data, results should be presented and critically discussed. This often involves creating tables and figures. Figures are generally preferred, as they can summarize information that would otherwise require extensive text: one figure is worth a thousand words. Tables, however, may be more appropriate in specific cases, such as describing demographic characteristics.
When communicating results, two points should be emphasized:

Using descriptive statistics to summarize data, often through clear visualizations of metrics of interest.
Making claims based on group results7 using inferential statistics.

 AI-generated illustration created with ChatGPT (OpenAI), 2026
crumbs: Introduction to Experimental Design

title: Introduction to Experimental Design
section: Why the Trouble of Studying Statistics?
text:
Why the Trouble of Studying Statistics?
The concepts covered in this course go beyond data analysis. BEs with a solid understanding of experimental design and statistics are better positioned to secure attractive job opportunities. There are concrete examples of how this knowledge can be beneficial.



Assisting Companies in Making Profit
Following Susan White [1]:

To sell their products, a company must convince physicians that its products are better than those of competitors. To make its point, a company uses graphs, charts, and the results of studies comparing its products with others on the market.

Understanding how to present information is crucial. What graphical representation best conveys your message? How can you create effective visualizations?

This course will provide the tools to identify appropriate graphs and create effective visualizations.





Improving Clinical/Sport Practice
Biomedical Engineering spans a wide range of scales, from cellular to whole-body systems. Data collected to test hypotheses may therefore be highly diverse. Processing data is not sufficient; once metrics of interest are obtained, decisions must be made regarding the hypothesis. These decisions rely on probability.

This course will guide BEs in making informed decisions and conducting effective experiments.





Staying Up-To-Date
Understanding experimental design, statistical methods, and scientific reporting is essential for staying current with advancements. New findings are reported in peer-reviewed manuscripts8.
Staying up-to-date requires the ability to critically interpret findings. This includes understanding whether graphical results genuinely support the authors’ claims.
Consider, for example, the data shown in Figure 1, illustrating the relationship between peak power and cycling cadence.

Fresh group: subjects performed maximal efforts starting from rest.
Fatigued group: subjects performed the same trials after a fatiguing exercise.

Based on this information, try to address the questions: Does maximal peak power depend on pedaling rate? Does fatigue affect peak power?






Figure 1: This figure was created with ChatGPT (OpenAI), based on simulated data according to Beelen and Sargeant 1991 [3]. Circles and whiskers correspond to mean and standard deviation, respectively. * denote statistical significance at p < 0.05”
crumbs: Introduction to Experimental Design

title: Introduction to Experimental Design
text:
How is This Book Structured?
This book provides students with the theoretical and practical knowledge necessary to successfully complete the course on Experimental Design in Biomedical Engineering. Three main topics are covered, organized into three separate Parts:



Part 1: Smart Posture
The theory covered in this course will be applied to real data collected by each student9. To this end, one experimental paradigm has been selected: the control of human standing posture. After a brief overview of the fundamentals of postural control, postural sway data will be collected directly using a smartphone with the PhyPhox app. This part introduces the theoretical background and provides a tutorial on how to collect posture data using smartphones.




Part 2: R Tutorial
All practical activities in this course will be completed using the R programming language, hereafter referred to as R. The main features of R are covered in this part, including the Integrated Development Environment RStudio, as well as syntax, variable definition, data import/export, functions, and code automation.




Part 3: The Theory Behind Experimental Design
This part covers the theoretical aspects of experimental design, from study types to methods for hypothesis testing. All questions presented during lectures are included here in an interactive quiz format, allowing students to test their knowledge either during lectures or while studying at home.




[1] White SE. Basic & Clinical Biostatistics. Fifth Edition. New York, N.Y: McGraw-Hill Education; 2020.


[2] Mendoza MF, Sulague RM, Posas-Mendoza T, Lavie CJ. Impact of coffee consumption on cardiovascular health. Ochsner Journal 2023;23:152–8. https://doi.org/10.31486/toj.22.0073.


[3] Beelen A, Sargeant AJ. Effect of fatigue on maximal power output at different contraction velocities in humans. Journal of Applied Physiology 1991;71:2332–7. https://doi.org/10.1152/jappl.1991.71.6.2332.

title: 1  The Physiology of Human Standing Posture
text:
1.1 Why Study Posture Control?
There are two main reasons for selecting human posture control as the experimental paradigm used throughout this course.
Although some individuals exhibit exceptional balance abilities, others struggle to stand upright without external support. A limited ability to control standing posture compromises the performance of everyday activities and increases the risk of unintentionally coming to rest on the ground—that is, of falling.
Not surprisingly, the World Health Organization identifies falls as the second leading cause of unintentional injury deaths worldwide.
Falls occur more frequently in older adults. Even when not severe enough to require medical attention, falls may have important psychological consequences. For example, Vellas and colleagues [1] reported that approximately one-third of older adults develop a fear of falling after experiencing a fall.
While falls may result from environmental factors, there also appears to be a substantial intrinsic component associated with postural control. Robinovitch and coworkers [2] conducted a large observational study in Canadian long-term care facilities and reported that a considerable proportion of falls were attributable to internal causes rather than external hazards.
Importantly, this evidence does not imply a direct causal effect of age. Aging alone does not appear to reduce postural stability below the threshold associated with falling (Figure 1.1). Shupert and Horak [3] proposed that aging accounts for only a modest decline in postural stability. However, pathological conditions affecting the sensory-motor system1 may accelerate this decline and substantially increase fall risk.
Understanding the mechanisms underlying falls is not the only reason for studying posture control. Postural control is a ubiquitous requirement of daily life. Without it, we would be unable to perform actions as simple as sitting, standing, walking, catching a ball, swimming, or reading a book. Unlike voluntary movements of the upper limbs, however, postural control operates largely outside conscious awareness. We rarely think about the postural requirements imposed by a task, yet these requirements must constantly be met.

title: 1  The Physiology of Human Standing Posture
section: 1.2 Meeting Postural Demands Provides Evidence of Postural Control
text:
1.2 Meeting Postural Demands Provides Evidence of Postural Control
Consider holding a large box while standing. Upper-limb muscles must generate sufficient force to support the box, with the finger flexors and elbow flexors playing a primary role. At the same time, the box imposes substantial postural demands. Its weight generates forward-rotating moments about the ankle, hip, and trunk joints. If these moments were not compensated for, the body would be pushed forward by gravity and a fall could occur.
Our sensory-motor system continuously meets these demands. Consequently, muscles that are not directly involved in holding the box are activated to preserve stability, often without entering conscious awareness.
The need for postural control lies at the foundation of motor development. Following Figure 1.2, inspired by the work of Shumway-Cook and Wollacott [4], control of head posture in space, typically achieved around two months of age, develops before effective trunk control emerges. As coordinated control of the head and trunk matures, new functional skills such as sitting and crawling become possible. Independent standing represents a later developmental milestone, requiring coordinated activation of numerous muscles across multiple body segments.
Thus, the development of posture can be viewed as a progressive maturation of both segmental control and inter-segmental coordination.






Figure 1.2: Maturation of the inter-segmental control of posture. AI-generated illustration created with ChatGPT (OpenAI), 2026.
crumbs: Part 1: Smart Posture
1  The Physiology of Human Standing Posture

title: 1  The Physiology of Human Standing Posture
text:
1.3 The Definition of Postural Control
Posture refers to the configuration of body segments in space. Postural control can therefore be defined as the maintenance of the position and orientation of body segments relative to one another and to the environment.
It is useful to distinguish posture from balance. Posture describes body configuration, whereas balance (or postural stability) refers to the ability to maintain the body’s center of mass within the limits imposed by the base of support. Postural control encompasses the processes through which both posture and balance are achieved.
Postural control is highly dynamic and depends on task demands, environmental conditions, and individual characteristics.
Consider the task of reading a book while standing. Upper-limb muscles are required to hold the book in a comfortable reading position, whereas lower-limb and trunk muscles are required to maintain the upright stance. The postural demands associated with this task change according to:

Task: reading while seated imposes different requirements on the lower limbs and trunk.
Environment: reading while standing on a moving bus increases stability demands because external perturbations must be compensated for.
Individual: individuals with impaired postural control may not be able to read comfortably while standing.

While the first two aspects are associated with external factors, the latter is intrinsic to the individual and reflects that person’s ability to control posture.



There Is No Single System for Postural Control
Postural control does not rely on a single physiological system. Rather, it emerges from the coordinated interaction of multiple sensory and motor systems.
Physiological receptors provide information about body position, movement, and orientation. Muscles act as mechanical actuators, generating forces and moments that stabilize the body. Neural structures integrate sensory information and coordinate the activation of appropriate muscles in response to changing demands.
Several theories have been proposed to explain how these processes are organized. A detailed discussion of such theories is beyond the scope of this course. Instead, our goal is to provide the physiological background necessary for understanding how orientation-related signals can be measured and analyzed experimentally.
The following sections provide a brief overview of the motor and sensory components involved in postural control.




Regulation of Muscle Force
Active muscle force production is essential for maintaining upright posture. Force generation results from a cascade of electrochemical events occurring within the neuromuscular system.
At the cellular level, force is generated through interactions between the protein filaments actin and myosin within muscle fibers. These interactions are triggered by action potentials. When action potentials generated in and propagated along a motoneuron reach the neuromuscular junction, action potentials are subsequently generated within the muscle fibers it innervates.
These muscle-fiber action potentials propagate along the membrane and trigger contraction of individual sarcomeres, the fundamental contractile units of skeletal muscle. Tension rises rapidly following excitation and gradually declines as the membrane returns to its resting state.
The force response elicited by a single action potential is known as a twitch. A twitch is characterized by a relatively rapid contraction phase followed by a slower relaxation phase (Figure 1.3). Depending on the muscle considered, the complete twitch response may last several hundred milliseconds.






Figure 1.3: Schematic animation illustrating the generation of muscle force in response to a single motorneuron action potential.



By itself, the functional utility of a single twitch is limited. To meet the force requirements associated with posture and movement, the nervous system regulates force through two primary mechanisms.
Temporal Summation (Rate Coding). Force can be increased by reducing the interval between successive action potentials. As the discharge rate of a motoneuron increases, individual twitches overlap and sum, producing progressively larger forces (cf. Figure 1.4).









Low discharge rate








High discharge rate







Figure 1.4: Force response to trains of action potentials generated at different rates.



Spatial Summation (Recruitment). Force can also be increased by recruiting additional motoneurons. Because each motoneuron activates a set of muscle fibers, with motoneuron and the served muscle fibers being collectively known as a single motor unit, recruitment increases the number of fibers contributing to force production (cf. Figure 1.5).






Figure 1.5: Example of force modulation in response to the recruitment of motorneurons



Together, rate coding and recruitment provide the nervous system with powerful mechanisms for regulating muscle force over a wide range of functional demands.




Sensing Posture
Generating force is not sufficient for controlling posture. The nervous system must also determine when, where, and how much force should be applied. This requires continuous information about body position and orientation.
Before discussing the sensory systems involved in postural control, it is useful to consider the simple analogy shown in Figure 1.6. Imagine a child playing with an articulated doll and attempting to place it in a standing position. The child first manipulates the doll’s limbs and trunk until the desired posture is achieved. At this stage, the orientation goal has been met: the body segments have been arranged in the intended configuration.
The child then releases the doll and observes whether it remains standing. If the doll falls, the posture must be adjusted and the process repeated. In this way, achieving a stable standing posture becomes a trial-and-error process. Visual information allows the child to determine whether the desired posture has been maintained, while the hands provide the forces required to modify the orientation of the doll’s segments.






Figure 1.6: Analogy for understanding the key aspects involved in posture control, from sensing to acting. AI-generated illustration created with ChatGPT (OpenAI), 2026.



Although human postural control is vastly more sophisticated, the same fundamental principles apply. The nervous system must

continuously estimate the orientation of body segments in space through sensory signals that provide information about body orientation and movement.
coordinate the contraction of synergistic muscles to preserve stability.

The main difference is that, rather than relying solely on vision and conscious trial-and-error, the body integrates information from multiple sensory systems and automatically adjusts muscle activity to meet postural demands.
Three major sensory systems contribute to postural control:

Proprioceptive system, which provides information about joint position, muscle length, muscle force, and segment orientation. Based on the proprioceptive feedback, the nervous system is informed about the relative position and orientation of segments.
Vestibular system, which provides information about head motion and orientation relative to gravity.
Visual system, which provides information about the position and movement of the body relative to the surrounding environment.

Postural control depends on the integration of these sensory signals. The relative contribution of each source varies according to task demands and environmental conditions. For example, visual information becomes particularly important when navigating complex environments, whereas proprioceptive information is critical when standing on stable ground. The importance of each of these sensory channels, as well as to evaluate them, will be covered in the following chapter.






Note



Like the child in Figure 1.6, the nervous system must: 1) achieve a desired orientation of body segments and; 2) maintain that orientation against gravity.
Sensory information (vision, proprioception, vestibular) provides feedback, and motor actions generate the forces needed to preserve stability.

1  The Physiology of Human Standing Posture

title: 1  The Physiology of Human Standing Posture
text:
1.4 Evidence of Posture Control
Even though there is no specific physiological system dedicated to the control of posture, the existence of distinct postural strategies is well established. These strategies, defined as coordinated patterns of activation across groups of synergistic muscles, support the notion that postural control is an intrinsic component of the sensory-motor system. Evidence for postural strategies stems primarily from perturbation studies, in which systematic or random external disturbances are applied to the standing body.



Postural Strategies
The classic experiments conducted by Lewis Nashner provided pivotal contributions to our understanding of postural control. Nashner [5] observed that specific and reproducible sequences of muscle activation emerged in response to perturbations applied to the support surface. For example, forward translation of the support surface resulted in a disto-proximal sequence of muscle activation, beginning with the tibialis anterior and followed by the quadriceps muscles. Perturbations in the opposite direction elicited a temporally similar sequence of responses in the posterior muscles.
A legitimate question arises: was this coordinated muscle activity simply the result of stretch reflexes triggered sequentially as muscles were progressively stretched by the perturbation, starting from the most distal muscles? Or did it reflect an internally organized postural strategy, generated in response to a predictable perturbation?
In his study, Nashner [5] addressed this question by applying both translational and rotational perturbations of the support surface. Because rotational perturbations primarily stretched the ankle dorsiflexor muscles, only these muscles would be expected to respond if stretch reflexes alone accounted for the postural corrections observed during surface translation. Instead, Nashner observed that the sequential disto-proximal response was largely invariant to the type of perturbation, suggesting that such inter-segmental coordination of muscle activity is a hallmark of postural control.
The postural strategy reported by Nashner has proven to be clinically relevant. For example, it has successfully distinguished healthy individuals from patients with neurological disorders. As reported by Horak and colleagues [6], neurological patients respond to translational surface perturbations by stiffening the ankle and hip joints through muscle co-contraction2.




Ankle, Hip, and Step Strategies
Depending on the characteristics of the perturbation and the support surface, different postural strategies may be employed (cf. Figure 1.7).
When perturbations are small and the support surface is stable, muscles are activated in a disto-proximal sequence. Ankle muscles are activated first, followed by hip and trunk muscles. As a consequence, the body moves as a relatively rigid segment, resembling an inverted pendulum pivoted around the ankle joints. The direction of motion, forward or backward, depends on the direction and nature of the perturbation. This pendular movement of the body around the ankle characterizes the ankle strategy (panel 1 in Figure 1.7).
When standing on a narrow or compliant support surface, fast responses are required to preserve stability. Ankle-generated corrections are intrinsically slow and may become ineffective. In these circumstances, the coordinated pattern of muscle activation reverses direction, proceeding from proximal to distal muscles. As a result, movement occurs predominantly around the hip joint. The forward and backward motion of the trunk about the hip joint, achieved through a proximo-distal pattern of muscle activation, defines the hip strategy (panel 2 in Figure 1.7).
Finally, perturbations may exceed the corrective capabilities of both the ankle and hip strategies. In such cases, stability is restored by changing the base of support through a step. The step strategy is characterized by a movement of one or both legs in the direction of the perturbation, to avoid falling (panel 3 in Figure 1.7).






Figure 1.7: Well-established postural strategies used to preserve stability. AI-generated illustration created with ChatGPT (OpenAI), 2026.
crumbs: Part 1: Smart Posture
1  The Physiology of Human Standing Posture

title: 1  The Physiology of Human Standing Posture
text:
1.5 Assessing Posture Control
While perturbation-based experiments have greatly contributed to our understanding of postural stabilization mechanisms, postural control is most commonly assessed during quiet standing.
Because postural control relies on continuously estimating body orientation in space, many experimental studies focus on measuring variables related to body sway and segment orientation. These measurements provide indirect information about the sensory-motor processes underlying posture control and will form the basis of the experimental analyses developed in the following chapters.




[1] VELLAS BJ, WAYNE SJ, ROMERO LJ, BAUMGARTNER RN, GARRY PJ. Fear of falling and restriction of mobility in elderly fallers. Age and Ageing 1997;26:189–93. https://doi.org/10.1093/ageing/26.3.189.


[2] Robinovitch SN, Feldman F, Yang Y, Schonnop R, Leung PM, Sarraf T, et al. Video capture of the circumstances of falls in elderly people residing in long-term care: An observational study. The Lancet 2013;381:47–54. https://doi.org/10.1016/s0140-6736(12)61263-x.


[3] Shupert CL, Horak FB. Adaptation of postural control in normal and pathologic aging: Implications for fall prevention programs. Journal of Applied Biomechanics 1999;15:64–74. https://doi.org/10.1123/jab.15.1.64.


[4] Shumway-Cook A, Woollacott MH. Motor control : Translating research into clinical practice. 3rd ed. Philadelphia: Lippincott Williams & Wilkins; 2007.


[5] Nashner LM. Fixed patterns of rapid postural responses among leg muscles during stance. Experimental Brain Research 1977;30. https://doi.org/10.1007/bf00237855.


[6] Horak FB, Nutt JG, Nashner LM. Postural inflexibility in parkinsonian subjects. Journal of the Neurological Sciences 1992;111:46–58. https://doi.org/10.1016/0022-510x(92)90111-w.

1  The Physiology of Human Standing Posture

title: 2  Stabilometry
text:
2.1 The Orthostatic Posture
The objective assessment of postural control provides insights into deficits and improvements in balance performance. Clinicians and health scientists, for example, may use this information to guide rehabilitation programs aimed at preserving or restoring the control of orientation and stability. As discussed in the previous chapter, inadequate postural control compromises the successful execution of virtually all motor tasks.
The orthostatic posture is achieved by standing upright and quietly—it is often referred to as the quiet standing posture. While postural perturbations may provide valuable information about compensatory mechanisms, studying postural control during quiet standing allows us to probe the status quo of the systems involved in posture control.
Maintaining a stable quiet standing posture is the result of a complex sensory-motor integration process, the mechanisms of which are still debated. This apparently simple task imposes substantial stabilization demands that, if not met, may lead to a fall. The complexity arises from the need to keep the body center of gravity (CoG)1 above the relatively small base of support defined by the contact between the feet and the ground. Because of internal mechanical perturbations, such as respiration and the cardiac cycle, controlling CoG position is inherently a dynamic task. Indeed, as early as the 1930s, Hellebrandt [1] defined quiet standing as:
In the upright stance, the body CoG projects near most of the body joints. The vertical projection of the body CoG relative to the different joints is represented by the vertical line shown in the left panel of Figure 2.1. This vertical line defines the action of gravity relative to the joints and is referred to as the gravity line of action.
Stabilization is achieved by maintaining this line within the theoretical limits of the base of support. Close inspection of Figure 2.1 reveals that gravity produces a forward moment about most body joints, particularly the ankle joint, since the gravity line of action generally passes through or slightly anterior to them. Although the proximity of the gravity line to the joints minimizes torque demands, the CoG is not stationary during quiet standing. The gravity line continuously shifts forward, backward, and laterally, requiring the active regulation of muscle activity. The muscles most frequently involved in quiet-standing stabilization are shown in Figure 2.1.
Possibly because the gravity line is, on average, farther from the ankle joint than from any other major joint, the calf muscles are among the most extensively studied muscles during quiet standing. Composed primarily of the gastrocnemius and soleus, the calf musculature is a powerful muscle group responsible for generating plantar-flexion torque. In other words, it generates an extension moment about the ankle, either moving the toes downward or moving the body backward (cf. animation in Figure 2.1).
Completing the exercise below will help understand why plantar-flexor muscles are actively recruited to counteract the forward movement of the CoG in response to gravity.
The first evidence of the importance of assessing postural control during quiet standing likely stems from the observations of Romberg, a German neurologist. When deprived of vision, Romberg observed that some patients were unable to maintain a stable standing posture. This instability, characterized by exaggerated postural sway when standing with the eyes closed, was recognized as a manifestation of sensory ataxia2. Although subjective, the Romberg test remains clinically relevant for the assessment of balance disorders.
The orthostatic posture is therefore a dynamic task, characterized by the timely coordination of muscle activity to prevent falls. This process results in postural sway that, under normal conditions, is typically of millimetric magnitude. In pathological conditions, postural sway increases and may become visually apparent.
crumbs: Part 1: Smart Posture

title: 2  Stabilometry
text:
movement upon a stationary base.








Figure 2.1: The human quiet standing posture and a key muscle involved in standing stabilization—the gastrocnemius.












Can we readily tell which muscles are active and when?

flexors (\(\downarrow\)), extensors (\(\uparrow\))it is not possible to sayonly extensors (\(\uparrow\) and \(\downarrow\))only flexors (\(\uparrow\) and \(\downarrow\))flexors (\(\uparrow\)), extensors (\(\downarrow\))



It is not possible to say, given no information is provided about the gravity vector with respect to the segments shown.
If gravity vector was in the downward direction in the figure, the correct answer would be that elbow flexor muscles are contracting both in the up and down phase. In the up phase they would be producing positive work, moving the limb against gravity. In the down phase they would be producing negative work, obsorbing gravity work as the limb is moved down.












NoteAssessing postural sways



It is therefore conceivable that any approach capable of quantifying postural sway, or of providing metrics derived from postural sway, would be of major importance for the objective assessment of postural control.
crumbs: Part 1: Smart Posture
2  Stabilometry

title: 2  Stabilometry
text:
2.2 Measuring Postural Sways
From a simple plumb line hanging from the waist to sophisticated motion-capture systems, several techniques can be used to quantify postural sway. The most widely adopted approach relies on the measurement of ground reaction forces using dedicated force plates. Before detailing this technique, it is important to distinguish between the center of pressure (CoP) and the center of gravity (CoG).



CoG vs CoP
Consider the simple challenge of balancing a broom on the palm of the hand (Figure 2.2). The CoP, that is, the point where the broom force is applied to the support surface, is located on the palm where contact occurs between the broom handle and the hand. Although contact occurs over a finite area, the CoP is represented as a single point. The CoG, whose location depends on how mass is distributed within the broom, is located near the brush, where most of the broom mass is concentrated.
Balancing the broom requires continuously moving the CoP so that the motion of the CoG can be controlled. In the example shown in Figure 2.2, moving the hand rapidly forward would reverse the net moment acting on the broom, slowing its forward fall and initiating a backward movement. Similarly, moving the hand backward would reverse the direction of motion once again. By repeatedly adjusting the position of the hand, the CoG can be maintained within a limited range. The size of this range depends on how effectively CoP movements compensate for CoG movements.






Figure 2.2: Distinguishing center of gravity (CoG) from center of pressure (CoP)



A similar representation can be applied to the standing human body (Figure 2.2). During quiet standing, body motion can be reasonably approximated by an inverted pendulum pivoted around the ankle joint [2] [3]. Within this framework, gravity produces a forward moment about the ankle joint, which is counteracted by the extension torque generated by the plantar-flexor muscles (see Figure 2.1).
Contraction of the plantar-flexor muscles moves the CoP forward. Because the feet remain fixed to the ground, it can be shown that CoP displacement is proportional to ankle plantar-flexion torque [4]. If plantar-flexion torque exceeds the gravitational torque, determined by body weight (\(W_b\)) and by the horizontal position of the CoG relative to the ankle (\(y_{\text{cog}}\)), the CoG accelerates backward and a backward sway eventually begins. Conversely, reversing a backward sway requires moving the CoP behind the CoG. Interestingly, this may be achieved simply by reducing plantar-flexion torque. Contrary to common intuition, an active dorsiflexion torque may not be necessary to pull the body forward, as gravity alone may provide the required acceleration.
Consequently, stabilization of the body CoG results from a continuous modulation of CoP position. During quiet standing, a biomechanical constraint imposes a strong coupling between the displacement of these two quantities. As the CoG moves forward, the CoP also moves forward; as the CoG moves backward, the CoP follows in the same direction. Therefore, CoG and CoP displacements are strongly coupled and generally move in the same direction.






ImportantCoG vs CoP



It is clear that CoP and CoG are distinct but closely related constructs. This relationship justifies the measurement of CoP as an indirect approach for studying and evaluating postural control during quiet standing.






The Stabilometry Technique
Stabilometry is an objective technique used to quantify CoP position during quiet standing. It relies on computerized methods that process force signals measured at the support surface.
Two main systems can be used to compute CoP position: pressure insoles and force plates. Pressure insoles consist of a grid of pressure sensors embedded within shoe insoles. From the pressure distribution beneath the feet, the position of the CoP on the support surface can be estimated. Force plates, in contrast, consist of a rigid platform mounted on force-sensing elements. Force plates are generally preferred because they allow both vertical and horizontal force components to be considered in the computation of CoP position.
An example of a force plate, including the force-sensing elements, the measured forces, the resulting moments, and the reference system used for CoP computation, is illustrated in Figure 2.3. The resultant force applied to the force-plate surface is represented by the vector \(F\). The CoP corresponds to the location at which this resultant force acts on the plate.
Four force sensors are shown, one at each corner of the force plate. Each sensor provides a force vector (\(f_i, \, i=1,2,3,4\)), representing the ground reaction force measured at that location. Together, the four force vectors must reproduce the resultant force \(F\) and the moments generated by its point of application. Consequently, the magnitude and direction of the individual ground reaction forces depend on the magnitude, direction, and location of \(F\).






Figure 2.3: Example of a stabilometric trial (left) and of the quantities involved in the computation of CoP position (right).



In static equilibrium, the sum of all forces is equal to zero:
\[
\mathbf{F} + \mathbf{f_1}+\mathbf{f_2} + \mathbf{f_3} + \mathbf{f_4} = 0
\tag{2.1}\]
The physical principle used to measure force is generally based on either piezoelectric or strain-gauge technology. In the former case, the accumulation of electrical charge within each sensor is proportional to the force applied to it. In the latter case, force is inferred from changes in the electrical resistance of strain gauges. These changes result from deformation (strain) of the sensing element, which is proportional to the applied force.



Different force-plate manufacturers exist, and their systems typically provide direct access to the measured forces and moments.
Supposing the force plate you are using provides the three (\(x,y,z\)) components of each of the four force vectors shown in Figure 2.3, how would you compute the body weight (\(W_{\text{b}}\)) from these quantities?

by summing the magnitudes of the four force vectorsby summing the four vertical force components and taking the magnitude of the resultnot possible to compute because the body mass is not known



During quiet standing, the body is approximately in static equilibrium. Therefore, the sum of all external forces acting in the vertical direction must be equal to zero.
Let
\[
f_{z_i}, \, i=1,2,3,4
\]
be the vertical components of the four ground reaction forces measured by the force plate.
The only external forces acting in the vertical direction are:

the body weight (\(W_{\text{b}}\)), acting downward;
the vertical components of the ground reaction forces, acting upward.

Consequently,
\[
W_{\text{b}} + f_{z_1} + f_{z_2} + f_{z_3} + f_{z_4} = 0
\]
and therefore
\[
W_{\text{b}} =
|-(f_{z_1}+f_{z_2}+f_{z_3}+f_{z_4})|
\]
Thus, body weight can be computed from the sum of the vertical components of the ground reaction forces.







Computing CoP Position
The CoP position is computed from the ground reaction forces measured by the force plate (see Figure 2.3). The computation procedure is detailed below for the anterior-posterior direction (\(y_{\text{cop}}\)), that is, in the sagittal plane.








Figure 2.4: CoP computation in the anterior-posterior direction.






Figure Figure 2.4 illustrates a parasagittal view of a person standing on a force plate, including the measured vertical ground reaction forces and the resultant force. During quiet standing, and under the assumption that horizontal accelerations are negligible, the resultant vertical force can be approximated by the body weight:
\[
\mathbf{F} \approx \mathbf{W_{\text{b}}}.
\tag{2.2}\]


Given this approximation, the computation can be introduced by considering only the vertical components of the ground reaction forces:
\[
f_{z_i}, \, i=1,2,3,4.
\]
The figure illustrates \(f_{z_3}\) and \(f_{z_4}\) with greater magnitude than \(f_{z_1}\) and \(f_{z_2}\) only to make the four vertical forces visually clear. Depending on the CoP position, the opposite configuration may occur. For example, \(f_{z_1}\) may be greater than \(f_{z_3}\), just as \(f_{z_2}\) may be greater than \(f_{z_4}\).
By considering only vertical forces, the computation of CoP position becomes relatively simple. First, a reference system for representing the CoP coordinates must be defined. In Figures 2.3 and 2.4, the center of the force-plate surface is taken as the origin of the reference system, with the \(x\), \(y\), and \(z\) axes directed respectively to the right, anteriorly, and upward.
Mathematically, \(y_{\text{cop}}\) is determined from the balance of moments around the medio-lateral axis. In static equilibrium, the sum of the moments is equal to zero:
\[
\mathbf{M_x} + \mathbf{M_1} + \mathbf{M_2} + \mathbf{M_3} + \mathbf{M_4} = 0
\tag{2.3}\]
where \(\mathbf{M_x}\) is the moment produced by the resultant force \(\mathbf{F}\) around the medio-lateral axis (\(x\) axis in Figure 2.3), and \(\mathbf{M_i}\), with \(i=1,2,3,4\), is the moment produced by each ground reaction force about the same axis.
Each moment is given by the cross product between the position vector and the corresponding force vector. For the four vertical forces measured by the sensors, we have:
\[
\begin{aligned}
M_1 &= (-\Delta_y\hat{\mathbf{u}}_y) \times \mathbf{f_{z_1}} = -\Delta_y f_{z_1}\hat{\mathbf{u}}_x\\
M_2 &= (+\Delta_y\hat{\mathbf{u}}_y) \times \mathbf{f_{z_2}} = +\Delta_y f_{z_2}\hat{\mathbf{u}}_x\\
M_3 &= (-\Delta_y\hat{\mathbf{u}}_y) \times \mathbf{f_{z_3}} = -\Delta_y f_{z_3}\hat{\mathbf{u}}_x\\
M_4 &= (+\Delta_y\hat{\mathbf{u}}_y) \times \mathbf{f_{z_4}} = +\Delta_y f_{z_4}\hat{\mathbf{u}}_x,
\end{aligned}
\tag{2.4}\]
where \(\Delta_y\) is the distance between each sensor measuring point and the origin of the reference system, projected on the \(y\) axis. The vectors \(\hat{\mathbf{u}}_x\) and \(\hat{\mathbf{u}}_y\) are unit vectors in the corresponding axes, while \(f_{z_i}\) denotes the magnitude of the vertical reaction force measured by the \(i\)-th sensor.
In an analogous way, the moment produced by the body weight in the sagittal plane is given by:
\[
\mathbf{M_x} = \mathbf{y}_{\text{cop}} \times \mathbf{W_{\text{b}}} = -y_{\text{cop}}W_{\text{b}}\hat{\mathbf{u}}_x,
\tag{2.5}\]
where \(y_{\text{cop}}\) is the anterior-posterior coordinate of the point of application of \(\mathbf{W_{\text{b}}}\) on the support surface, and \(W_{\text{b}}\) is the magnitude of the body weight vector.
Substituting the expressions above into the moment equilibrium equation 2.3 and rearranging terms yields:
\[
y_{\text{cop}} = \Delta_y\frac{f_{z_2}+f_{z_4}-f_{z_1}-f_{z_3}}{W_{\text{b}}}.
\]
Because, under static equilibrium, body weight can be estimated from the sum of the vertical reaction forces, \(W_{\text{b}}\) can be replaced by:
\[
W_{\text{b}} = f_{z_1}+f_{z_2}+f_{z_3}+f_{z_4}.
\]
Therefore, the anterior-posterior CoP coordinate is computed as:
\[
y_{\text{cop}} = \Delta_y\frac{f_{z_2}+f_{z_4}-f_{z_1}-f_{z_3}}{f_{z_1}+f_{z_2}+f_{z_3}+f_{z_4}}.
\tag{2.6}\]
Likewise, the CoP position in the frontal plane is defined as:
\[
x_{\text{cop}} = \Delta_x\frac{-f_{z_2}+f_{z_4}-f_{z_1}+f_{z_3}}{f_{z_1}+f_{z_2}+f_{z_3}+f_{z_4}},
\tag{2.7}\]
where \(\Delta_x\) is the projection of the distance between each sensor and the origin of the reference system on the \(x\) axis.






NoteWhat about the horizontal reaction forces?



The CoP equations presented above neglect the horizontal ground reaction forces. This approximation is generally adequate during quiet standing because horizontal forces are relatively small.
When greater accuracy is required, however, the horizontal force components can be incorporated into the computation. The correction depends on the horizontal reaction forces and on the vertical distance between the force sensors and the surface on which the subject stands (i.e. the force-plate thickness).






Stabilogram and Statokinesigram
The animation in Figure 2.5 provides a representation of the excursion of the CoP over the force plate during quiet standing—a stabilometric trial. Green circles correspond to the CoP coordinates \((x_{\text{cop}}, y_{\text{cop}})\) computed from the ground reaction forces. The distance between consecutive positions depends on both the sampling frequency of the acquisition system and the CoP speed. In stabilometric recordings, sampling frequencies are typically greater than 20 Hz [5].






Figure 2.5: Example of a stabilometric trial, showing the CoP excursion during quiet standing.



Stabilometric systems often provide users with the time series of the CoP position in both the medio-lateral (\(x\)) and anterior-posterior (\(y\)) axes. Otherwise, CoP position must be computed from ground reaction forces or from moments. Some systems provide the forces measured by each sensor, whereas others provide the three components of the resultant force (cf. vector \(F\) in Figure 2.3) and the moments it produces.
Once a stabilometric trial is collected, the CoP profile may be inspected either in time or in space. These two forms of representation are shown in Figure 2.6.
Stabilograms correspond to the temporal representation of CoP position during a stabilometric trial. Two stabilograms are obtained from each trial: one associated with CoP position in the medio-lateral direction and the other associated with CoP position in the anterior-posterior direction.
Statokinesigrams combine the CoP excursion in both axes into a single Cartesian plot. Even though the statokinesigram provides a spatial representation of the CoP samples, it depends on the recording duration. The longer the recording duration, the greater the number of CoP samples used to create the statokinesigram.






Figure 2.6: Two possible representations of CoP excursion. The statokinesigram represents CoP displacement in space, whereas the stabilograms represent the medio-lateral and anterior-posterior CoP coordinates over time.







CoP Metrics
In any field of study, metrics of interest should be extracted from the specific signal collected (e.g., ECG, EMG, stabilograms). These metrics should be physiologically meaningful or, at least, provide a tangible interpretation of the process under investigation.
Several metrics may be computed from both stabilograms and statokinesigrams. Traditional, or classic, metrics rely mainly on basic statistics. More than 30 commonly used classic metrics are listed by Prieto et al. [6]. Other metrics are based on more advanced signal-processing techniques, such as the sway density curve [7] or fractional Brownian motion [8].
For the purposes of this course, three classic metrics will be considered. Two metrics will be computed from stabilograms: the CoP standard deviation and the CoP mean speed. Both are computed separately for the medio-lateral and anterior-posterior axes. A third metric, the elliptical area, will be computed from the statokinesigram.



Stabilogram Metrics (\(\sigma\) and \(v\))
The CoP standard deviation is defined by:
\[
\sigma_x = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left(x_{\text{cop}}(i)-\bar{x}_{\text{cop}}\right)^2}
\tag{2.8}\]
and
\[
\sigma_y = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left(y_{\text{cop}}(i)-\bar{y}_{\text{cop}}\right)^2}
\tag{2.9}\]
where \(N\) is the total number of samples, and \(\bar{x}_{\text{cop}}\) and \(\bar{y}_{\text{cop}}\) are the mean CoP positions on the two anatomical axes:
\[
\bar{x}_{\text{cop}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}x_{\text{cop}}(i)
\]
and
\[
\bar{y}_{\text{cop}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}y_{\text{cop}}(i).
\]
The mean speed is computed from consecutive CoP displacements, separately for the medio-lateral and anterior-posterior axes:
\[
v_x = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{i=1}^{N-1}\left|x_{\text{cop}}(i+1)-x_{\text{cop}}(i)\right|
\tag{2.10}\]
and
\[
v_y = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{i=1}^{N-1}\left|y_{\text{cop}}(i+1)-y_{\text{cop}}(i)\right|
\tag{2.11}\]
where \(T\) is the duration of the stabilometric trial. The absolute value is used because, otherwise, forward and backward displacements, or leftward and rightward displacements, would cancel each other.
These two metrics have distinct interpretations. The CoP standard deviation quantifies the average size of the region in the horizontal plane within which postural sway occurs. The CoP mean speed provides an indication of how rapidly corrections to postural sway are made—for example, how rapidly the hand moves when balancing the broom in Figure 2.2. Biomechanically, CoP standard deviation reflects how much ankle torque is modulated, whereas CoP mean speed reflects how rapidly ankle torque changes.




Statokinesigram Metric (\(EA\))
While CoP standard deviation assesses the size of postural sway separately along each anatomical direction, the area of the statokinesigram combines the medio-lateral and anterior-posterior excursions into a single global measure of sway size.
The idea is to compute the size of the region containing the CoP samples. Because CoP displacement during quiet standing is irregular, computing the boundary of this region analytically is not straightforward. A practical alternative is to approximate the statokinesigram using a geometric shape and then compute the area of that shape. The question is which shape should be used.
Using a circle or a non-rotated rectangle to approximate the statokinesigram is generally not appropriate. As shown in the left panel of Figure 2.7, these shapes may substantially overestimate the region actually occupied by the CoP samples. Rotating a rectangle according to the direction along which CoP displacement is mainly distributed provides a more representative approximation. However, the area is still overestimated, because large empty regions, especially near the corners of the rectangle, are included in the computation.
In 1996, Oliveira and coworkers [9] proposed a statistical approach to compute the area of the statokinesigram. The method is based on identifying two principal axes defining an ellipse that contains a large fraction of the CoP samples. An example is provided in the right panel of Figure 2.7. Compared with circular or rectangular approximations, the ellipse adapts both to the orientation and to the anisotropy of the CoP cloud.






Figure 2.7: Different approaches for computing the CoP area. The left panel compares circular and rectangular approximations, whereas the right panel illustrates the elliptic area based on the principal axes of CoP displacement.



The statistical ellipse is obtained from the covariance matrix of the CoP samples:
\[
\Sigma =
\begin{pmatrix}
\sigma_x^2 & \sigma_{x,y} \\
\sigma_{y,x} & \sigma_y^2
\end{pmatrix},
\]
where \(\sigma_x^2\) and \(\sigma_y^2\) are the CoP variances in the medio-lateral and anterior-posterior directions, respectively, and \(\sigma_{x,y}=\sigma_{y,x}\) is the covariance between CoP positions in the two directions.
The eigenvalues of this covariance matrix, denoted by \(\lambda_1\) and \(\lambda_2\), define the variance of the CoP samples along the two principal axes of the ellipse. They are computed as:
\[
\lambda_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2}{2}
\pm
\sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x^2 - \sigma_y^2}{2}\right)^2 + \sigma_{x,y}^2}.
\]






NoteVariance, covariance, and eigenvalues



The concepts of variance and covariance will be addressed in more detail later in the course. They are introduced here only to explain how the major and minor axes of the ellipse are computed from the statokinesigram.


The square roots of the eigenvalues provide the standard deviations of the CoP distribution along the principal axes of the ellipse. Following the convention represented in Figure 2.7, the semi-axis lengths are obtained by multiplying these standard deviations by 1.96:
\[
a = 1.96\sqrt{\lambda_1}
\]
and
\[
b = 1.96\sqrt{\lambda_2},
\]
where \(a\) and \(b\) are the semi-major and semi-minor axes of the ellipse, respectively. The elliptic area is then computed as the area of an ellipse:
\[
EA = \pi ab = \pi1.96^2\sqrt{\lambda_1\lambda_2}
\tag{2.12}\]
The elliptic area provides a compact descriptor of the global spatial dispersion of CoP samples. Larger values indicate that the CoP explored a larger region of the support surface during quiet standing. In this sense, \(EA\) complements the stabilogram metrics: while \(\sigma_x\), \(\sigma_y\), \(v_x\), and \(v_y\) describe sway separately along each anatomical direction, \(EA\) summarizes the overall size of the statokinesigram in the horizontal plane.






NoteWhy 1.96?



As will become clear later in the course, the factor 1.96 corresponds to the 95% confidence interval of a normally distributed variable. Multiplying the principal-axis standard deviations (\(\sqrt{\lambda_1}\) and \(\sqrt{\lambda_2}\)) by 1.96 therefore defines the major and minor axes of a 95% confidence interval.
Because both directions are considered simultaneously, the resulting ellipse encloses approximately 85% of the CoP samples [9].
crumbs: Part 1: Smart Posture
2  Stabilometry

title: 2  Stabilometry
text:
2.3 From Theory to Practice (Smart Posture)
At this point, we have developed an understanding of what postural sway is and how it can be quantified from CoP measurements. This information provides the basis for studying the physiology of postural control under a wide range of conditions, including motor training, environmental perturbations, pathological conditions, and quiet standing. Likewise, factors not directly related to the physiology of postural control can also be investigated, such as intention [10], stabilometric trial duration [11], and anthropometric characteristics [12].
Although this course is not about posture control itself, stabilometry provides an excellent experimental paradigm for learning the principles of experimental design and statistical inference. Throughout the course, postural sway measurements will serve as the experimental data upon which the inferential methods introduced in later chapters will be applied. Consequently, data collection constitutes a fundamental component of the learning experience.






Because collecting stabilometric trials from every student using a laboratory-grade force plate would be impractical, postural sway data will be collected using the inertial sensors embedded in modern smartphones.


Unlike force plates, smartphones do not provide direct measurements of the CoP. Instead, they record body accelerations and angular velocities, from which orientation-related signals can be derived. For the purposes of this course, these signals will be assumed to provide a meaningful representation of postural sway, allowing students to collect, analyze, and interpret experimental data using readily available technology.




[1] Hellebrandt FA. STANDING AS a GEOTROPIC REFLEX: T<scp>he</scp> m<scp>echanism of the</scp> a<scp>synchronous</scp> r<scp>otation of</scp> m<scp>otor</scp> u<scp>nits</scp>. American Journal of Physiology-Legacy Content 1938;121:471–4. https://doi.org/10.1152/ajplegacy.1938.121.2.471.


[2] Winter DA, Patla AE, Prince F, Ishac M, Gielo-Perczak K. Stiffness control of balance in quiet standing. Journal of Neurophysiology 1998;80:1211–21. https://doi.org/10.1152/jn.1998.80.3.1211.


[3] Morasso P, Cherif A, Zenzeri J. Quiet standing: The single inverted pendulum model is not so bad after all. PLOS ONE 2019;14:e0213870. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0213870.


[4] Morasso PG, Schieppati M. Can muscle stiffness alone stabilize upright standing? Journal of Neurophysiology 1999;82:1622–6. https://doi.org/10.1152/jn.1999.82.3.1622.


[5] Vieira TMM, Oliveira LF, Nadal J. Estimation procedures affect the center of pressure frequency analysis. Brazilian Journal of Medical and Biological Research 2009;42:665–73. https://doi.org/10.1590/s0100-879x2009000700012.


[6] Prieto TE, Myklebust JB, Hoffmann RG, Lovett EG, Myklebust BM. Measures of postural steadiness: Differences between healthy young and elderly adults. IEEE Transactions on Biomedical Engineering 1996;43:956–66. https://doi.org/10.1109/10.532130.


[7] Jacono M, Casadio M, Morasso PG, Sanguineti V. The sway-density curve and the underlying postural stabilization process. Motor Control 2004;8:292–311. https://doi.org/10.1123/mcj.8.3.292.


[8] Collins JJ, De Luca CJ. Open-loop and closed-loop control of posture: A random-walk analysis of center-of-pressure trajectories. Experimental Brain Research 1993;95:308–18. https://doi.org/10.1007/bf00229788.


[9] Oliveira LF, Simpson DM, Nadal J. Calculation of area of stabilometric signals using principal component analysis. Physiological Measurement 1996;17:305–12. https://doi.org/10.1088/0967-3334/17/4/008.


[10] Stoffregen TA, Pagulayan RJ, Bardy BG, Hettinger LJ. Modulating postural control to facilitate visual performance. Human Movement Science 2000;19:203–20. https://doi.org/10.1016/s0167-9457(00)00009-9.


[11] Carpenter MG, Frank JS, Winter DA, Peysar GW. Sampling duration effects on centre of pressure summary measures. Gait & Posture 2001;13:35–40. https://doi.org/https://doi.org/10.1016/S0966-6362(00)00093-X.


[12] Chiari L, Rocchi L, Cappello A. Stabilometric parameters are affected by anthropometry and foot placement. Clinical Biomechanics 2002;17:666–77. https://doi.org/10.1016/s0268-0033(02)00107-9.

title: 3  PhyPhox SmartPosture Tutorial
text:
3.1 What is PhyPhox?
This part of the course provides students with the opportunity to work with their own experimental data. The selected experimental paradigm is postural control. Briefly, postural sway data will be collected under different standing conditions and subsequently used as a benchmark for applying the statistical methods presented later in the course.
As discussed in Chapter 2, stabilometry is considered the gold standard technique for assessing postural sway. Stabilometric analysis is based on changes in the position of the body’s center of pressure (CoP), which are estimated from ground reaction forces.
Collecting stabilometric data during class would be impractical, particularly for a large group of students. Therefore, taking advantage of the widespread availability of smartphones, this course adopts a smartphone-based approach for acquiring postural sway data.
Although students are free to use any application they consider appropriate, all practical activities in this course will rely on the PhyPhox initiative.
PhyPhox is an educational application developed at RWTH Aachen University, Germany. It was designed to enable students to perform scientific experiments using their smartphones, effectively transforming the device into a portable physics laboratory.
With PhyPhox, it is possible to investigate a wide variety of physical phenomena, including:
PhyPhox operates through experiments, which correspond to predefined sets of instructions governing data acquisition. Users may either select one of the built-in experiments or create custom experiments using the PhyPhox web editor.
crumbs: Part 1: Smart Posture
3  PhyPhox SmartPosture Tutorial

title: 3  PhyPhox SmartPosture Tutorial
crumbs: Part 1: Smart Posture
3  PhyPhox SmartPosture Tutorial

title: 3  PhyPhox SmartPosture Tutorial
text:
3.2 PhyPhox and Postural Sways
For the purposes of this course, a custom PhyPhox experiment—named the SmartPosture Experiment—was developed specifically for measuring postural sway.
The SmartPosture experiment provides an indirect estimate of postural sway based on signals acquired from three smartphone sensors: the accelerometer, gyroscope, and magnetometer. Body sway is estimated from changes in smartphone orientation about its \(x\) and \(y\) axes. According to the reference system illustrated in Figure 3.1, the following convention applies:

Sway in the sagittal plane is estimated from smartphone rotation about the \(x\) axis. Thus, pitch reflects the antero-posterior displacement (\(y_{cog}\)) of the body’s center of gravity (CoG).
Sway in the frontal plane is estimated from smartphone rotation about the \(y\) axis. Accordingly, roll reflects the medio-lateral displacement (\(x_{cog}\)) of the body CoG.







Figure 3.1: Reference system for measuring postural sways



More specifically, smartphone rotations about the \(x\) and \(y\) axes are estimated using the orientation virtual sensor1.
Rotations in the sagittal plane are represented by \(\theta\) (pitch), whereas rotations in the frontal plane are represented by \(\gamma\) (roll). The coordinates of the body center of gravity (CoG) projected onto the horizontal plane are therefore given by
\[
\begin{aligned}
x_{cog}(t) &= \gamma(t) \times h \\
y_{cog}(t) &= -\theta(t) \times h
\end{aligned}
\tag{3.1}\]
The negative sign ensures that a counterclockwise rotation about the \(x\) axis corresponds to a forward body sway. The parameter \(h\) represents the height of the body CoG above the ground (see Figure 2.2), estimated from the anthropometric data reported by Winter [1]:
\[
h \approx 0.55 \times b_\text{h}
\]
where \(b_\text{h}\) denotes total body height.






NoteBeware of the assumptions



Two important assumptions underlie the use of Equation 3.1 to estimate postural sway from smartphone orientation.
First, body rotations are assumed to be sufficiently small for the approximation \(sin \theta \approx \theta\) to remain valid.
Second, the smartphone must be held in portrait orientation, with its \(z\) axis aligned with the direction of gravity (see Figure 3.2).








ImportantValidity of the assumptions



During quiet standing, body movements are typically small enough for both assumptions to be considered valid.
For the educational purposes of this course, the postural sway data estimated from Equation 3.1 provide an appropriate framework for learning the processing of stabilometric data and statistical analysis.










Figure 3.2: How to hold the smartphone.




During quiet standing, the arms should hang naturally alongside the body, while the head should remain in a neutral position (neither flexed nor extended).
Although the posture illustrated in Figure 3.2 does not exactly correspond to that adopted during a standard stabilometric assessment, it is presented solely to facilitate data acquisition without requiring assistance from another person.
Hold the smartphone firmly and avoid moving any body segment immediately after starting data acquisition (see the experimental protocol below).
crumbs: Part 1: Smart Posture
3  PhyPhox SmartPosture Tutorial

title: 3  PhyPhox SmartPosture Tutorial
section: 3.3 Installing PhyPhox and the SmartPosture Experiment
text:
3.3 Installing PhyPhox and the SmartPosture Experiment
To download the PhyPhox app, use the appropriate link below according to the operating system installed on your smartphone.









The next step is to import the SmartPosture experiment into PhyPhox. To do so, follow the four steps illustrated in Figure 3.3 and described below.






Figure 3.3: Steps for importing the SmartPosture experiment into the PhyPhox collection.






Step 1: RunStep 2: AddStep 3: Save



Launch the PhyPhox App
When you launch PhyPhox, the home screen displays a list of the built-in experiments available in the application. Because SmartPosture is a custom experiment, it must first be imported. Press the \(+\) icon located in the upper-right corner of the screen (see panel 1 in Figure 3.3).




Import the SmartPosture Experiment


PhyPhox provides three different methods for importing experiments, all illustrated in panel 2 of Figure 3.3. The simplest approach is to scan the QR code shown in panel 3 and reproduced here for convenience.








Save the Experiment to Your Collection
After scanning the QR code, PhyPhox will automatically download and load the SmartPosture experiment.






Because this is the first time the experiment has been loaded, PhyPhox will display a prompt asking whether the remotely hosted experiment should be added to your collection. Select Save to collection so that the experiment becomes permanently available from the home screen.






After the SmartPosture experiment has been successfully added to your collection, it will appear in the PhyPhox home screen and will be ready for use.
crumbs: Part 1: Smart Posture
3  PhyPhox SmartPosture Tutorial

title: 3  PhyPhox SmartPosture Tutorial
text:
3.4 Running the SmartPosture Experiment
This section introduces the basic features of the SmartPosture experiment and explains how to use the application to collect postural sway data.



Launching SmartPosture
After completing the installation procedure described in the previous section, the SmartPosture experiment will appear in your PhyPhox experiment collection.






Launch PhyPhox and open the SmartPosture experiment. It should be listed under the Biomedical tab on the PhyPhox home screen, as illustrated in the figure.






SmartPosture Interface
The SmartPosture experiment comprises two main screens, each providing a different representation of the stabilometric data (see Figure 2.6). These screens are illustrated in Figure 3.4.






Figure 3.4: The two screens of the SmartPosture experiment.




Graphs
The Stabilogram screen displays two time-series graphs. The upper graph shows CoG displacement in the medio-lateral (frontal plane) direction, whereas the lower graph displays CoG displacement in the antero-posterior (sagittal plane) direction.
The Statokinesigram screen displays a single graph showing the trajectory of the body CoG projected onto the horizontal plane.


Slider
The slider available on both screens should be adjusted according to the participant’s height, expressed in centimeters. This value is used to scale the smartphone rotations, thereby estimating the horizontal projection of the body CoG according to Equation 3.1.


Dropdown Menus
Two dropdown menus allow the user to specify the base of support and the standing condition adopted during the stabilometric trial. Each menu provides three available options.



The base of support is defined according to foot position during the trial.

wide: feet positioned approximately hip-width apart.
narrow: feet positioned close together.
tandem: one foot positioned directly in front of the other.





The standing condition specifies the experimental condition under which the trial is performed.

eyes open: standing quietly with the eyes open.
eyes closed: standing quietly with the eyes closed.
still: standing while deliberately minimizing postural sway.





Icon Buttons
Three icon buttons are available in the upper-right corner of the screen.

 Play: starts and stops data acquisition. When pressed, the play icon changes to a pause icon while data are being recorded.
 Clear: removes all CoG samples currently stored in memory.
 Settings: opens the configuration menu for the current PhyPhox experiment.





Exporting Data
After completing a stabilometric trial with the SmartPosture experiment, the recorded CoG data can be exported and stored locally on your smartphone. The export procedure is illustrated in Figure 3.5.
After stopping data acquisition, press the Settings button. A menu will appear containing several options, including Export Data. Select this option to open the export menu. Among the available export formats, choose Excel, and then press Export Data in the bottom-right corner of the screen. PhyPhox will then prompt you to choose where the exported file should be saved, either locally on the device or in a cloud storage service.






Figure 3.5: Exporting CoG data in SmartPosture.
crumbs: Part 1: Smart Posture
3  PhyPhox SmartPosture Tutorial

title: 3  PhyPhox SmartPosture Tutorial
text:
3.5 The Experimental Protocol
After installing PhyPhox and importing the SmartPosture experiment into your PhyPhox collection, collect postural sway data according to the protocol below.

Position yourself as illustrated in Figure 3.2 while holding the smartphone.
Collect postural sway data using the SmartPosture interface by following the sequence of steps (a to h) described below and illustrated in the accompanying video (Figure 3.6).
Repeat the procedure for each combination of base of support (wide, narrow, and tandem) and standing condition (eyes open, eyes closed, and still).







a: Launch the SmartPosture experiment.
b: Select either the Statokinesigram or Stabilogram visualization.
c: Set your height.
d: Select the appropriate base of support.
e: Select the standing condition.
f: Assume the required posture and press the Play button.
g: Maintain the standing position for 40 s under the selected base of support and standing condition.
h: Export the data in Excel format before performing any other action.





Video


Figure 3.6: Steps for collecting postural sway data with SmartPosture.












ImportantWhat’s next?



At the end of the experiment, you should have nine Excel files corresponding to the nine combinations of base of support (3) and standing condition (3).
Upload all nine files to the Moodle platform. Instructions for the submission procedure will be provided during class.






[1] Winter DA. Biomechanics and motor control of human movement. Wiley; 2009. https://doi.org/10.1002/9780470549148.

title: 4  Example Data: Postural Sways
text:
Getting Started with R
All concepts dealt with during this course apply to the design of experiments involving biomedical data. This tutorial, and all practical exercises, are structured to provide an overview on the R programming language within one of the many contexts of relevance in biomedical studies: the control of the human standing posture.
In dedicated lectures we briefly went through some techniques for assessing posture control, with stabilometry being the gold standard. This technique consists in the use of a force plate to measure ground reaction forces, from which the CoP coordinates are calculated. From the CoP coordinates we then compute some metrics that are expected to be informative on the integrity of the posture control system: see Chapter 2 for an overview.
It is unfeasible to obtain CoP data for all students in this course. However, benefiting from the virtual sensor1 present in our smartphones, all students will have the opportunity to collect orientation data associated with their postural sways. #sec-ch13 provides detailed guidelines on the experimental procedures.
The Part 2 of this book (R Tutorial) is structured according to the following Chapters:
Instructions on how to install and to start using R are given.
crumbs: Part 2: R Tutorial
4  Example Data: Postural Sways

title: 4  Example Data: Postural Sways
text:
The Basics with R
A brief, though exhaustive, overview is provided on how R works. Pieces of R code introduced in this Chapter are based on arbitrary values of CoG mean speed \(v_x\) in the lateral direction (left-right; see Equation 2.10). In particular, we suppose to have obtained \(v_x\) values on five fictitious subjects, according to the Table 4.1.




Table 4.1: Fictitious data for a group of subjects.












Subject
Gender
Age
\(v_x \text{ (m/s)}\)
Parkinsonian2




Sarah
female
31
6.1
FALSE


Rob
male
25
5.2
FALSE


Ted
male
28
5.7
FALSE


Jessica
female
47
7.1
TRUE


John
male
30
6.3
FALSE










Plotting and Processing Raw Data
It may be that we download or obtain the variables directly from a repository, from previously collected data, or by any other means. However, it is often the case that we need to compute these metrics from raw data, collected by ourselves or by the team we work with. In this Chapter we see how R can be used to process raw postural sway data to compute three variables (see Section 2.2.5):

Elliptic Area (\(EA\)). Indicates how largely our body moves in the horizontal plane.
Mean Speed (\(v_{x,y}\)). How fast the our body moves, separately along the frontal (medio-lateral direction) and sagittal (anterior-posterio direction) planes.
Standard Deviation (\(\sigma_{x,y}\)). Reflects how large the postural sways are, separately in the frontal and sagittal planes.





Automating Raw Data Processing
Regardless of the problem we are dealing with, we will need to obtain variables for a number of experimental units (typically subjects in biomedical studies), often in different conditions. If processing raw data is required to obtain the variables, we therefore would need to do that for every single experimental unit and condition. In this case, it is advisable to automate the processing of raw data. First because it saves time and second because it minimizes errors due to manual processing. In this Chapter, we see how to automate the processing of postural sway raw data in R.
