title: 5  Getting Started with R
text:
5.1 Installation (R and RStudio)
All activities in this course will be conducted using the R programming language. R is free, open source, and was originally developed for statistical computing and data visualization.
As stated on the R Project website, R is
The same source further emphasizes that:
R has become one of the standard languages for statistical analysis in biomedical research, as reflected by its widespread use in scientific reports and peer-reviewed articles.
Start by following the two steps indicated on the Posit website:
RStudio is an open-source integrated development environment (IDE) for using R, although it can also be used with other languages, such as Python. For students who do not wish to install the desktop version, RStudio can also be used through Posit Cloud2.
crumbs: Part 2: R Tutorial
5  Getting Started with R

title: 5  Getting Started with R
text:
install R
install RStudio
crumbs: Part 2: R Tutorial
5  Getting Started with R

title: 5  Getting Started with R
text:
5.2 RStudio IDE
After R and RStudio have been correctly installed, RStudio can be launched. The RStudio IDE interface should then appear, with four main panes, as shown in Figure 5.1.
The following sections provide a brief explanation of the features of interest in each pane.






Figure 5.1: RStudio IDE Interface






Pane 1

This pane may not appear when RStudio is launched for the first time. It displays readable files, such as txt, md, Rmd, qmd, css, json, tex, yml files, among others. Of particular interest are R files, which are plain-text files containing executable R code. We will return to R files in Chapter 7.
Briefly, the Source Pane is where users can view and edit code-related files.





Pane 2
This pane is used to execute R code interactively. In the example shown in Figure 5.1, a numeric variable named x has been created and the value 1 has been assigned to it. Notice that, unlike in many other programming languages, the operator <- is commonly used for assignment in R instead of =. Chapter 6 introduces some basic R features, including variable definition, data types, data structures, and data import.




Pane 3
There are two main tabs of interest in this Pane:

Environment
The Environment tab displays all currently saved R objects. After running the command x <- 1 in Pane 2 (Console), the variable x is saved in the R Global Environment. From the moment the variable x has been defined, it can be inspected in the Environment tab (cf. Pane 3 in Figure 5.1).
Other potentially useful features of the Environment tab include:

interactive import () of datasets from text files, Excel files, or common statistical software formats (e.g., SPSS).
display of memory usage.
the brush icon (), which removes all objects from the workspace (Global Environment).
loading and saving () data into and from the R workspace.



History
In this tab, all commands executed during the current R session can be inspected. It is also possible to save and load history data, as well as to erase the entire history or only part of it.





Pane 4
There are four main tabs of interest in this Pane:

Files
This tab is self-explanatory. It allows users to navigate through directories, create new folders or files, delete files, and rename them. One important concept introduced here is the Working Directory. This is the directory R uses when executing code, and it is not necessarily the same directory currently shown in the Files tab.
To set a Working Directory, click on the () icon on the top-right edge of the Files tab. Then, navigate to the directory you wish to set as your Working Directory, select it, click on the () icon, and then click on Set As Working Directory (Figure 5.2).
The importance of the Working Directory will be discussed in Chapter 7.






Figure 5.2: Setting current directory as the Working Directory





Plots
All static figures created during an R session are stored in this tab. Users can navigate through the figures created, export them, delete all or some of them, and zoom in or out. This tab provides a useful graphical interface for exploring datasets.


Packages
Packages are R libraries containing functions, datasets, documentation, and, in some cases, compiled code. Packages extend the capabilities of R for specific tasks, such as data manipulation, visualization, or inferential statistics.
The Packages tab lists all installed packages. A package can be used only after it has been installed. To install a new package, most of which are available from the Comprehensive R Archive Network, use the command install.packages().


Help
In this tab, users can find documentation for packages and functions.
You can navigate through previously opened help pages using the left and right arrows (). By clicking on the home icon (), the general help page appears, providing many useful links.
To open the documentation for a specific function, you may either use the help(function_name) command or the syntax ?function_name. Consider, for example, the function mentioned in the previous section, install.packages(). To check its documentation, you may type either:

?install.packages
help(install.packages)
crumbs: Part 2: R Tutorial

5  Getting Started with R

title: 6  The Basics with R
text:
6.1 Objects
Before delving into the many statistical and graphical tools available for data analysis, we first need to become familiar with the basic logic of the R programming language. This is the purpose of this tutorial.
Everything stored in R is an object. An object may be a variable, a function, a dataset, or any other element created during an R session. Objects available in the R workspace can be inspected either by typing ls() in the Console or by checking the Environment tab.
The object shown in the Environment tab (Figure 6.1) was created after running the code described in the following section.
crumbs: Part 2: R Tutorial
6  The Basics with R

title: 6  The Basics with R
text: Figure 6.1: Checking existing variables.
crumbs: Part 2: R Tutorial
6  The Basics with R

title: 6  The Basics with R
text:
6.2 Variables
Variables can be created using either the = or <- operator. Although both operators can be used for assignment, there are functional differences between them. For example, <- has higher precedence than =, which may lead to unexpected results if both are mixed in the same line of code. The general recommendation in the R Style Documentation is to use <- for assignment:

Most usages of the equal sign for assignments do not cause runtime errors. Not all possible problems were eliminated, however. Thus the equal sign is not recommended, it is tolerated.

We will therefore use <- for assignments and = for specifying named arguments within functions. Examples will follow.
Let us create the variable mu_cog_x_speed, storing the mean of the five CoG speed values listed in Table 4.1. We will then check whether this variable is present in the R workspace and print its value:

mu_cog_x_speed <- (6.1 + 5.2 + 5.7 + 7.1 + 6.3) / 5 # creating a variable object in R
print(mu_cog_x_speed)                               # printing the value of the only object created

[1] 6.08



ls()                                                # listing objects in the workspace



[1] "mu_cog_x_speed"


The hash symbol # indicates that the text following it is a comment, not a piece of code to be executed.
The variable just created also appears in the Environment tab (Figure 6.1). To remove this variable from the R workspace, we can use the command rm() (e.g., rm("mu_cog_x_speed")), or click on the brush icon () in the toolbar of the Environment tab. These options can be used to remove one, several, or all variables from the workspace.
crumbs: Part 2: R Tutorial
6  The Basics with R

title: 6  The Basics with R
text:
6.3 Functions
Functions work as they do in other programming languages: they are blocks of code that execute when called by the user. Built-in functions are not shown in the workspace, whereas custom functions created by the user are. We will return to custom functions later on in this tutorial.
Available functions and their documentation can be inspected under the Packages and Help tabs.
We will now compute the same mean value as before, this time using the mean() function available in the base package.

mu_cog_x_speed <- mean(c(6.1, 5.2, 5.7, 7.1, 6.3))
mu_cog_x_speed

[1] 6.08


There are two important points to notice here.

The computed mean value, stored in the variable mu_cog_x_speed, can be inspected without explicitly using the print() command.
The function mean() was applied to a vector containing the numbers 6.1, 5.2, 5.7, 7.1, and 6.3, which were concatenated with the c() function.
crumbs: Part 2: R Tutorial
6  The Basics with R

title: 6  The Basics with R
text:
6.4 Data types
The following data types are commonly used in R.



NumericIntegerCharacterLogical


This is the most common data type in R. Whenever decimal numbers are assigned to a variable, R stores them as numeric values by default. We can check this by creating the vector cog_x_speed, containing five plausible values for CoG speed in the lateral direction, using the c() function:

cog_x_speed <- c(6.1, 5.2, 5.7, 7.1, 6.3) # mm/s
class(cog_x_speed)

[1] "numeric"

str(cog_x_speed)

 num [1:5] 6.1 5.2 5.7 7.1 6.3


We used the class() and str() functions to inspect the type of the cog_x_speed variable. Notice that str() displays the data type, the dimension of the object, and the values stored in it.




Integer variables are similar to numeric variables, except that decimal values are not allowed. Suppose we want to store the age of the five subjects for whom cog_x_speed was computed. These values can be stored in a variable named age:

age <- as.integer(c(31, 25, 28, 47, 30)) # years
str(age)

 int [1:5] 31 25 28 47 30


By default, the vector created with the c() function stores numbers as numeric values. We explicitly converted them to integers using the function as.integer().




Character variables store text, also referred to as strings. Character values are created using quotation marks. In this example, we store the names of the five subjects for whom age and CoG speed values were obtained:

subjects <- c("Sarah", "Rob", "Ted", "Jessica", "John") # volunteers
str(subjects)

 chr [1:5] "Sarah" "Rob" "Ted" "Jessica" "John"






Logical variables can assume only two possible values: TRUE or FALSE. For example, we may ask whether each subject tested is younger than 30 years:

young_subjects <- age<30
young_subjects

[1] FALSE  TRUE  TRUE FALSE FALSE


According to this definition, two subjects are younger than 30. If we wish to identify these subjects, we can use the logical values stored in young_subjects as indices:

subjects[young_subjects]

[1] "Rob" "Ted"


In this case, R retrieves the second and third entries of the subjects variable because the second and third elements of young_subjects are TRUE. Alternative ways to access these two subjects are:

subjects[2:3]

[1] "Rob" "Ted"

subjects[c(2,3)]

[1] "Rob" "Ted"


The Parkinsonian column in Table 4.1 provides another example of a logical variable. We will recreate this variable below in the List section.
crumbs: Part 2: R Tutorial
6  The Basics with R

title: 6  The Basics with R
text:
6.5 Data structures
There are different ways to organize data into R objects, depending on whether the variables of interest store numeric, text, or other types of data. Three useful functions help us understand the differences between data structures in R:

typeof(), which indicates the type of the stored data.
length(), which returns the number of elements stored.
attributes(), which returns additional information about the object.

The following sections briefly describe the most relevant data structures used in this course.



Vectors
Vectors are one-dimensional objects storing a collection of elements, all of the same data type. In other words, vectors are homogeneous data structures. A vector can store numeric data, such as the values stored in the cog_x_speed variable created earlier, or character data, such as subject names:

subjects <- c("Sarah","Rob","Ted","Jessica","John")
str(subjects)

 chr [1:5] "Sarah" "Rob" "Ted" "Jessica" "John"

typeof(subjects)

[1] "character"


The two variables subjects and cog_x_speed store collections of elements of the same type, character and numeric, respectively. In R, vectors may have one important attribute: names. Names can be defined when using the c() function:

two_subjects <- c(Sarah = 6.1, Rob = 5.2) # or c("Sarah" = 6.1, "Rob" = 5.2)
two_subjects

Sarah   Rob 
  6.1   5.2 

attributes(two_subjects)

$names
[1] "Sarah" "Rob"  

names(two_subjects)

[1] "Sarah" "Rob"  

str(two_subjects)

 Named num [1:2] 6.1 5.2
 - attr(*, "names")= chr [1:2] "Sarah" "Rob"


Here, we used the names() function to retrieve the names of the entries in the vector two_subjects. The same function can also be used to define or modify the names attribute.




Matrices
Like vectors, matrices store data of a single type, but they arrange these data in two dimensions. Instead of storing age and CoG speed in two separate variables, we can create a matrix storing both variables in two separate columns, using the matrix() function:

age_and_cog_x_speed <- matrix( 
  data = c(31, 25, 28, 47, 30, 6.1, 5.2, 5.7, 7.1, 6.3), 
  nrow = 5, ncol = 2,
  byrow = FALSE             # indicates whether entries should be arranged by rows
  ) 

typeof(age_and_cog_x_speed) # why is "double" the result of typeof()?

[1] "double"

age_and_cog_x_speed

     [,1] [,2]
[1,]   31  6.1
[2,]   25  5.2
[3,]   28  5.7
[4,]   47  7.1
[5,]   30  6.3


The result of typeof() is "double" because all elements in a matrix must have the same type. Since the vector used to create the matrix contains decimal values, R stores all entries as numeric double-precision values, including the ages.
Similarly, we may name either or both dimensions of the matrix just created, using the functions dimnames(), rownames(), or colnames():

colnames(age_and_cog_x_speed) <- c("age","cog_x_speed")
attributes(age_and_cog_x_speed)

$dim
[1] 5 2

$dimnames
$dimnames[[1]]
NULL

$dimnames[[2]]
[1] "age"         "cog_x_speed"

str(age_and_cog_x_speed)

 num [1:5, 1:2] 31 25 28 47 30 6.1 5.2 5.7 7.1 6.3
 - attr(*, "dimnames")=List of 2
  ..$ : NULL
  ..$ : chr [1:2] "age" "cog_x_speed"








NoteUtility of matrices



Matrices will rarely be used to analyze results from biomedical research directly. They can be very useful, however, when processing raw data. We will use matrices later to compute stabilometric descriptors from the sway data collected with smartphones.






List
The main distinction between a list and a vector or matrix is that a list can store data of different types. A list can also nest vectors or matrices of different sizes.
With the list() function, we can create a list grouping the data shown in Table 4.1. This can be done without naming the groups of elements:

# creating a list with subjects, CoG lateral speed, and Parkinsonian status 
subjects <- c("Sarah", "Rob", "Ted", "Jessica", "John") 
cog_x_speed <- c(6.1, 5.2, 5.7, 7.1, 6.3)
parkinsonian <- c(FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE, FALSE)
table_1 <- list(subjects, cog_x_speed, parkinsonian)
table_1

[[1]]
[1] "Sarah"   "Rob"     "Ted"     "Jessica" "John"   

[[2]]
[1] 6.1 5.2 5.7 7.1 6.3

[[3]]
[1] FALSE FALSE FALSE  TRUE FALSE

typeof(table_1) # type is "list", as different data types are stored in table_1

[1] "list"

length(table_1) # this is 3, as there are 3 elements in table_1

[1] 3

str(table_1) # this fully describes table_1

List of 3
 $ : chr [1:5] "Sarah" "Rob" "Ted" "Jessica" ...
 $ : num [1:5] 6.1 5.2 5.7 7.1 6.3
 $ : logi [1:5] FALSE FALSE FALSE TRUE FALSE


In this case, each element in the list table_1 stores five values. This does not have to be the case. Different elements in a list may store different numbers of values, either of the same type or of different types.
It is worth noticing that we could have assigned names to each element in the list:

table_1 <- list(subjects = subjects, cog_speed = cog_x_speed, is_parkinsonian = parkinsonian)
str(table_1)

List of 3
 $ subjects       : chr [1:5] "Sarah" "Rob" "Ted" "Jessica" ...
 $ cog_speed      : num [1:5] 6.1 5.2 5.7 7.1 6.3
 $ is_parkinsonian: logi [1:5] FALSE FALSE FALSE TRUE FALSE

attributes(table_1)

$names
[1] "subjects"        "cog_speed"       "is_parkinsonian"






Factor
A factor is a special data type in R. It is used to represent categorical data, typically stored as repeated text labels across different observations1. *In our example, observations are the five subjects, who may be categorized, for example, according to gender**:

gender <- factor(c("female", "male", "male", "female", "male"))
str(gender)

 Factor w/ 2 levels "female","male": 1 2 2 1 2


In this case, the gender factor() has five entries and two levels—that is, the set of values the factor may assume: "male" and "female". The levels of a factor can be accessed with the levels() function:

attributes(gender)

$levels
[1] "female" "male"  

$class
[1] "factor"

levels(gender)

[1] "female" "male"  


We may add this factor to the table_1 list just created. Notice that we need to wrap it in another list before appending it; otherwise, we would not obtain the desired result. What would happen in that case?

table_1 <- append(table_1, list(gender = gender), after = 1) # insert at specific location
table_1

$subjects
[1] "Sarah"   "Rob"     "Ted"     "Jessica" "John"   

$gender
[1] female male   male   female male  
Levels: female male

$cog_speed
[1] 6.1 5.2 5.7 7.1 6.3

$is_parkinsonian
[1] FALSE FALSE FALSE  TRUE FALSE

str(table_1)

List of 4
 $ subjects       : chr [1:5] "Sarah" "Rob" "Ted" "Jessica" ...
 $ gender         : Factor w/ 2 levels "female","male": 1 2 2 1 2
 $ cog_speed      : num [1:5] 6.1 5.2 5.7 7.1 6.3
 $ is_parkinsonian: logi [1:5] FALSE FALSE FALSE TRUE FALSE


Notice that we used the append() function instead of c(). To remain consistent with the columns in Table Table 4.1, we used after = 1 to place gender as the second element of table_1.
Also notice how clearly the str() function summarizes the content of the table_1 object, indicating the number of elements stored, the name of each element (which could be NULL), the type of data, the number of values, and the corresponding values within each entry.
crumbs: Part 2: R Tutorial
6  The Basics with R

title: 6  The Basics with R
text:
6.6 Data Frames
A data frame is another R structure for organizing data. Instead of describing it alongside the other structures covered in the previous section, it is presented in a stand-alone section. This is because data frames are the most common data organization structure used in R for statistical analysis.
Data frames are tabular structures—that is, they organize data in two dimensions. Rows correspond to observations, while columns store the variables2 associated with each observation.
An observation is generally defined as a piece of information collected experimentally. In biomedical research, observations often correspond to persons. Taking the example of Table 4.1, which is redrawn here (cf. Table 6.1) to explicitly indicate the observations, experimental data were obtained from five subjects. The variables of interest are Gender, Lateral CoG Speed, and whether the subject is affected by Parkinson’s disease. Subject and Age store personal data3: the name of each individual is rarely useful in statistical analysis, whereas age may be relevant when evidence suggests that it may influence the variables of interest (e.g. does CoG speed depend on age?).





Table 6.1: Interactive table illustrating how a data frame looks.














Creating Data Frames
Table 6.1, from columns Subject to Parkinsonian, can be created using the data.frame() command, based on the R variables we created in previous sections:

df <- data.frame(subjects, gender, age, cog_x_speed, parkinsonian)
df

  subjects gender age cog_x_speed parkinsonian
1    Sarah female  31         6.1        FALSE
2      Rob   male  25         5.2        FALSE
3      Ted   male  28         5.7        FALSE
4  Jessica female  47         7.1         TRUE
5     John   male  30         6.3        FALSE


The tabular structure of the data frame object df is clear. Importantly, rows were identified with integers, from 1 to 5, and columns were named after the R variable objects passed to the data.frame() function. The name of each variable (column) may be defined explicitly when calling data.frame(). Using short but meaningful names is advisable.

df <- data.frame(sub = subjects, sex = gender, age = age, cog = cog_x_speed, disease = parkinsonian)
str(df)

'data.frame':   5 obs. of  5 variables:
 $ sub    : chr  "Sarah" "Rob" "Ted" "Jessica" ...
 $ sex    : Factor w/ 2 levels "female","male": 1 2 2 1 2
 $ age    : int  31 25 28 47 30
 $ cog    : num  6.1 5.2 5.7 7.1 6.3
 $ disease: logi  FALSE FALSE FALSE TRUE FALSE


The str() function provides a useful summary of the whole data frame structure. It tells us:

how many observations we have (\(n=5\)),
the variables associated with each observation,
the name of each variable ("sub", "sex", "age", "cog", "disease"),
the type of data stored in each column (chr, Factor, int, num, logi),
the data itself.





Accessing Entries in Data Frames
Elements stored for a single variable in the data frame df can be retrieved in different ways, using single brackets [], double brackets [[]], or the accessor $.
With single brackets [], we access one specific variable of the data frame df—that is, one of its five columns:

df[1] # this is a data frame with one variable ("sub") and five ("chr") observations

      sub
1   Sarah
2     Rob
3     Ted
4 Jessica
5    John

df[3] # this is a data frame with one variable ("age") and five ("int") observations

  age
1  31
2  25
3  28
4  47
5  30

str(df[3])

'data.frame':   5 obs. of  1 variable:
 $ age: int  31 25 28 47 30


To retrieve the entries of a single column as a vector, use double brackets [[]] or the accessor $:

df[[3]] # vector of integers 

[1] 31 25 28 47 30

str(df[[3]])

 int [1:5] 31 25 28 47 30

df$age # equivalent to df[[3]]

[1] 31 25 28 47 30






Retrieving Information About Data Frames
Some useful R functions can be used to inspect the structure of a data frame:

nrow(df) # number of rows (observations)

[1] 5

ncol(df) # number of columns (variables)

[1] 5

dim(df[1:3]) # number of rows and columns

[1] 5 3

length(df) # number of rows or columns? What do you think?

[1] 5


Often, a data frame contains many observations. In such cases, printing the full content of the data frame (df in our case) may be confusing, and we may lose sight of the column names. The head() function can help:

head(df, 3) # checking the first three observations in df

    sub    sex age cog disease
1 Sarah female  31 6.1   FALSE
2   Rob   male  25 5.2   FALSE
3   Ted   male  28 5.7   FALSE


The summary() function is remarkably useful for obtaining a brief overview of descriptive statistics for the whole df object. We will discuss descriptive statistics in detail later (cf Chapter 10). In our example, we have five variables, with the first serving only for illustrative purpose. For each of the following four variables we can quickly inspect:

Gender: the levels of this factor and the number of observations in each level,
Age and Lateral CoG Speed: minimum and maximum values, first, second, and third quartiles, and the mean value,
Parkinsonian: how many subjects are affected by Parkinson’s disease and how many are not.

This is achieved with summary():

summary(df)

     sub                sex         age      
 Length:5           female:2   Min.   :25.0  
 Class :character   male  :3   1st Qu.:28.0  
 Mode  :character              Median :30.0  
                               Mean   :32.2  
                               3rd Qu.:31.0  
                               Max.   :47.0  
      cog        disease       
 Min.   :5.20   Mode :logical  
 1st Qu.:5.70   FALSE:4        
 Median :6.10   TRUE :1        
 Mean   :6.08                  
 3rd Qu.:6.30                  
 Max.   :7.10
crumbs: Part 2: R Tutorial
6  The Basics with R

title: 6  The Basics with R
text:
6.7 Importing data
We will import data frequently, often with the purpose of addressing questions of interest, such as:

Does this new training program improve gait function?
Is muscle spasticity relieved by a serious-game rehabilitation program?
How much does this new method for counting motor units outperform existing approaches?

Addressing these questions requires the use of statistical tools to analyze variables. These variables may be obtained directly or after processing raw data for a group of subjects, according to a carefully planned experimental protocol. We may therefore have to import two types of files: files with raw data4 and files with variables5.






NoteWhich data will we work with?



For now, we will consider the former case, working with raw data. Upon completion of this tutorial, instead of working with simulated data or data collected by others, we will work with postural data collected by ourselves and from ourselves.





Getting Folder and File storing CoG data
In this course, PhyPhox is used to collect CoG data (cf Chapter 3).
The first step is to identify the folder where the raw data of interest are stored. For now, we are working with an example of CoG data, which you can download and find inside this compressed here.
Unzip the downloaded file and place its contents inside a folder named phyphox_data, in your Working Directory (cf. Files section in the previous Chapter).

my_wd <- getwd() # this command retrieves my working directory my_wd
my_wd
list.dirs(my_wd, recursive = FALSE, full.names = FALSE) # with list.dirs() we list the files inside our working directory 

The function list.dirs() listed all directories in my_wd, retrieved with the getwd() function. We see that there is only one phyphox_data directory inside my_wd. We may further check the content of phyphox_data:

list.files(file.path(my_wd, "phyphox_data"))

[1] "standing_eyes_closed"     "standing_eyes_closed.xls"
[3] "standing_eyes_open"       "standing_eyes_open.xls"  


With file.path(), we created a path to the phyphox_data directory by concatenating "phyphox_data" to my_wd. With the list.files() function, we listed the full content of the phyphox_data directory, including files and directories.
There are two xls files and two directories inside phyphox_data. All were obtained after exporting data from PhyPhox (see Section 3.4.2.0.5). Each folder is associated with an xls file—that is, the file "standing_eyes_open.xls" contains the same information as the file "postural_sways.csv" inside the folder "standing_eyes_open":

list.files(file.path(my_wd, "phyphox_data", "standing_eyes_open"))

[1] "meta"               "postural_sways.csv"






Loading CoG data into R workspace
To import CoG data into R, we may proceed interactively by right-clicking on the csv (or xls) file under the Files tab. Two options appear: View File and Import Dataset... (Figure Figure 6.2). By clicking on View File, a preview of the data is shown in the Source Pane if it is a csv file. If it is an xls file, R opens the file in Excel.






Figure 6.2: Interactive data import.



By clicking on Import Dataset..., a dialog may appear asking the user to install a package for importing the data, if the package is not already installed. If the required package is available, a window opens with useful and self-explanatory information on the data import procedure (Figure @ref(fig:figure-2-3-3)).






Figure 6.3: Interactive data import.



For now, we will import the csv data using the read.csv() function, already available in the utils package. The content of the csv file is loaded into the R workspace as a data frame. In the next Chapter, we will plot and process these raw CoG data, extracting the variables of interest for further analysis.

df <- read.csv(file.path(getwd(), "phyphox_data", "standing_eyes_open", "postural_sways.csv"))


str(df)

'data.frame':   3997 obs. of  3 variables:
 $ time : num  0.00769 0.0177 0.02771 0.03772 0.04772 ...
 $ y_cog: num  -82.2 -82.1 -82.1 -82.1 -82.1 ...
 $ x_cog: num  5.45 5.23 4.99 4.86 4.81 ...

head(df,5)

         time     y_cog    x_cog
1 0.007691333 -82.23271 5.452531
2 0.017699333 -82.11261 5.226466
3 0.027707333 -82.05521 4.994301
4 0.037715333 -82.05740 4.857281
5 0.047723333 -82.07852 4.807784

6  The Basics with R

title: 6  The Basics with R
text:
Exercise
Install the readxl package and use it to import raw CoG data from the "standing_eyes_open.xls" file, available in the phyphox_data folder. The function of interest is read_xls(). Use the head() function to check whether the first five rows of raw CoG data match the values listed above.
crumbs: Part 2: R Tutorial
6  The Basics with R

title: 6  The Basics with R
text:
Each observation corresponds to one or more pieces of information collected from a single experimental unit—typically a person in biomedical experiments.↩︎
The meaning of variable here should not be confused with the meaning of an object in R. Here, variable refers to the characteristic or attribute describing the observed or measured data.↩︎
Collecting personal data is discouraged. Only when the study requires keeping track of personal data should this possibility be considered.↩︎
Kinematic, kinetic, electrophysiological, or any other type of data (e.g. movies, light intensity, audio, etc.) collected for each individual.↩︎
Variables may be obtained by processing raw data (e.g., CoG speed) or collected directly from the experimental units. It is based on these metrics that we may test our hypotheses.↩︎
crumbs: Part 2: R Tutorial
6  The Basics with R

title: 7  Plotting and Processing Raw Data
text:
7.1 Important Considerations
Visual inspection and signal processing are beyond the scope of this course. Instead, our focus is on selecting the appropriate statistical tests and applying them correctly to draw inferences about the hypotheses under investigation. These tests require collecting information from a set of experimental units (e.g., participants, motor units, muscle fibers, or any other unit of analysis). Throughout this course, we will refer to this information as variables.
Some variables can be obtained by directly asking participants (e.g., age) or through simple measurements (e.g., body mass and height). Others require processing biomedical signals. In this tutorial, and throughout the exercises in this course, we will work with variables computed from postural sway data (CoG raw data; see Chapter 4).
Before processing any data, we must ensure that the quality of the raw measurements is sufficiently high—this is essential in any experiment or study. If you are unsure how to assess data quality, consult the scientific literature in your field and critically evaluate the characteristics of your measurements.
In our case, we collected smartphone orientation data while participants stood quietly. To assess the quality of the recorded data, it is useful to consider the following questions:
Once we have plausible answers to these—and possibly other—questions, we can visually inspect the data for artifacts. This prior understanding helps us recognize whether the recorded signals are consistent with the expected physiological behavior.
We will begin by loading the raw data from a csv file into the R workspace, as described in the Loading CoG data section.
df <- read.csv(file.path(getwd(), "phyphox_data", "standing_eyes_open", "postural_sways.csv"))
crumbs: Part 2: R Tutorial
7  Plotting and Processing Raw Data

title: 7  Plotting and Processing Raw Data
text:
How was the smartphone held during data acquisition?
Which reference coordinate system does the smartphone use to report orientation data?
Was the smartphone held firmly enough that changes in its orientation primarily reflect body sway rather than hand movements?
What is the expected amplitude of body sway under the different test conditions (e.g., eyes open, eyes closed)?
What frequency range should these sways exhibit? In other words, what are reasonable durations for the fastest and slowest body sway movements?
crumbs: Part 2: R Tutorial
7  Plotting and Processing Raw Data

title: 7  Plotting and Processing Raw Data
text:
7.2 Inspecting Raw Data
We have just loaded the contents of the file "postural_sways.csv" into the R workspace. This file contains three columns:

time: time instants at which the smartphone orientation data were recorded;
x_cog: CoG coordinate in the frontal plane1 (medio-lateral direction);
y_cog: CoG coordinate in the sagittal plane2 (antero-posterior direction).

If the data were collected as described in Chapter 3, x_cog and y_cog should represent body sway in the medio-lateral and antero-posterior directions, respectively.
To visualize the CoG position in the sagittal plane, we can use plot():

plot(df$y_cog)







Figure 7.1: The simplest plot of raw CoG Y data.






Helpful Cosmetics
By calling the plot() function, you may have noticed that R automatically labels both axes. The \(y\) axis is labeled according to the variable passed as the second argument (df$y_cog), while the \(x\) axis is labeled Index. After checking that we have 3997 data points using length(df$y_cog), it becomes clear that Index refers to the sample number, ranging from 1 to 3997.
Another feature worth noting is that each data point is represented by a marker—a black circle with a white fill. This default behavior is appropriate because R was originally designed for statistical analyses involving relatively small sets of observations, typically corresponding to the number of experimental units in a study (often fewer than 20). In our case, however, the CoG measurements form a continuous time series, making a line plot without markers a more appropriate representation.
These and many other graphical settings can be customized when calling the plot() function:

plot(df$time, df$y_cog,                          # x and y data respectively
     type = "l",                                 # line type plot
     col = "turquoise4",                         # color of the line
     main = "Stabilogram in the Sagittal Plane", # title text, shown on the top of the plot
     font.main = 2,                              # use bold font for the title text
     xlab = "Time (s)",                          # label text for the X axis
     ylab = "CoG Y Position (cm)", # label text for the Y axis
     bty = "l",                    # show only left and bottom borders
     )







Figure 7.2: Setting some plot cosmetics.



For a complete list of graphical parameters, consult the documentation for the par() function.
The anatomical interpretation of changes in CoG Y Position—that is, positive values correspond to forward displacement—was added using the arrows() and text() functions, which are omitted from the code listing for simplicity.




Plotting CoG X and Y
Stabilograms are graphical representations of postural sway as a function of time. Since body sway is measured in both the antero-posterior and medio-lateral directions, two stabilograms are available: one for the sagittal plane (Figures 7.1 and 7.2) and one for the frontal plane.
There are two common ways to display these stabilograms.



Stacking Stabilograms
One option is to create a separate plot for each stabilogram. Simply calling the plot() function twice would not achieve the desired result because R would create each plot in a separate graphics device (or overwrite the existing plot, depending on the environment). Instead, we would like to display both stabilograms within the same figure window. This can be accomplished using the layout() function.

desired_layout <- matrix(
  data = c(1, 2), 
  nrow = 2, 
  ncol = 1
  ) # where to plot the data

desired_layout  # column vector, indicating where to place plot 1 (top) and 2 (bottom)

layout(mat = desired_layout) # tell R to draw the next 2 plots with this layout

layout.show(2) # show the layout (outlines) of the next two plots







Figure 7.3: Defining plotting layout.



desired_layout is a matrix with one column and two rows, containing the values 1 and 2. These values specify where each plot should be drawn: the first plot occupies the top row and the second plot the bottom row. Since both plots are intended to have the same size, passing only the matrix desired_layout to the layout() function is sufficient.
We can now call plot() twice to draw the stabilograms for the sagittal and frontal planes.


Show code
layout(mat = desired_layout) # tell R to draw the next 2 plots with this layout

# first plot - CoG Y stabilogram
plot(df$time, df$y_cog, 
  type = "l", 
  col = "turquoise4", 
  xlab = "Time (s)", 
  main = "Stabilograms in the Sagittal and Frontal Planes",
  font.main = 2, 
  ylab = "CoG Y Position (cm)", 
  bty = "l"
  )

# second plot - CoG X stabilogram
plot(df$time, df$x_cog, 
  type = "l", 
  col = "purple4", 
  xlab = "Time (s)", 
  ylab = "CoG X Position (cm)", 
  bty = "l"
  )








Figure 7.4: Stacked stabilograms.







Overlaying Stabilograms
An alternative approach is to display both stabilograms in the same graph. In this case, there is no need to define a plotting layout. We first create one plot with the plot() function and then add the second trace using the lines() function3.


Show code
# first plot - CoG Y stabilogram
plot(df$time, df$y_cog, 
  type = "l", 
  col = "turquoise4", 
  xlab = "Time (s)", 
  main = "Stabilograms in the Sagittal and Frontal Planes",
  font.main = 2, 
  ylab = "CoG Position (cm)", 
  bty = "l", 
  ylim = range(c(df$x_cog,df$y_cog), na.rm = TRUE)
  )

# second plot - CoG X stabilogram
lines(df$time, df$x_cog, 
  type = "l", 
  col = "purple4", 
  bty = "l"
  )

# adding legend to distinguish the two traces
legend(
  "right", 
  inset = 0.02, 
  c("CoG Y","CoG X"), 
  col = c("turquoise4","purple4"), 
  lty = 1
  )








Figure 7.5: Overlaying stabilograms.



When overlaying plots, the different traces must be clearly distinguished and appropriately labeled. Here, different colors are used for the two signals, and the legend() function identifies which line corresponds to each CoG coordinate.
Unfortunately, the overlaid plot does not provide the intended visualization of postural sway in the sagittal (CoG Y) and frontal (CoG X) planes. The primary reason is that the two signals have substantially different mean values. To display both time series on the same graph, the limits of the \(y\) axis must be set to encompass the full range of both coordinates, namely [-82, 5], using the range() function (i.e., ylim = range(c(df$x_cog,df$y_cog), na.rm = TRUE)). Because the actual postural sway is only a few centimeters, scaling the \(y\) axis to a range of approximately 87 cm compresses both stabilograms, making the sway difficult to appreciate visually.
The mean value of each time series4—CoG Y and CoG X—is typically not of interest. It reflects the average position of the CoG during the trial rather than the magnitude or dynamics of body sway. In the next section, we will begin processing the raw data so that the two signals can be meaningfully overlaid and compared.
crumbs: Part 2: R Tutorial
7  Plotting and Processing Raw Data

title: 7  Plotting and Processing Raw Data
text:
7.3 Processing Raw Data
The appropriate procedure for processing raw data depends on both the characteristics of the data and the scientific question being addressed.
In this tutorial, we will work with postural sway data. Although no specific research question is investigated here, this tutorial provides an opportunity to become familiar with the RStudio IDE, which will be used throughout the course to address a variety of research questions.
The processing consists of two main steps:

Pre-processing Sway Data
Computing the Variables of Interest




Pre-processing Sway Data
Before computing any postural metric, we must ensure that the raw data have been properly prepared. Otherwise, the resulting metrics may have questionable validity. For example, recording duration is known to significantly influence postural metrics computed in both the time and frequency domains [1]. One way to minimize this effect is to standardize the duration of the recordings before computing the desired metrics.
In this tutorial, we will therefore perform two pre-processing procedures: trimming and demeaning.



Trimming Sway Data
From the plots shown earlier, we can see that approximately 40 s of sway data were recorded. The first few seconds are likely to contain transient effects (e.g., those associated with initiating data acquisition).
To standardize the duration of the recordings, we will retain a 30 s processing window. Specifically, we will discard the first 5 s of data and then keep the next \(N\) samples, where \(N = 30 \times sf\) and \(sf\) denotes the sampling frequency. Before doing so, we must determine the actual sampling frequency.
By default, PhyPhox records orientation data at approximately \(100\textrm{Hz}\) unless configured otherwise. Rather than assuming this nominal value, we will estimate the sampling frequency directly from the recorded timestamps.


Show code
si <- diff(df$time) # sampling intervals: between consecutive samples

plot(si*1000, 
  ylab = "Sampling interval (ms)", 
  xlab = "Sampling interval number (#)", 
  main = "Interval between consecutive samples", 
  font.main = 2, 
  col = "turquoise4"
  )








Figure 7.6: Checking sampling frequency consistency.



The intervals between consecutive samples are not perfectly constant, indicating a small amount of sampling jitter. On average, however, these deviations are on the order of \(1\mu\textrm{s}\), which is negligible compared with the duration of the 30 s processing window.
The estimated sampling frequency is slightly lower than \(100\textrm{ Hz}\). Based on Figure 7.6, the average sampling rate is \(1/0.010008=99.920064\textrm{ Hz}\). Consequently, assuming \(sf=100\textrm{ Hz}\) introduces a cumulative timing error of approximately \(+0.8\textrm{ ms}\) per second of processed data. For example, while 100 samples correspond to exactly \(1\textrm{ s}\) at \(100\textrm{ Hz}\), they actually span \(100/99.920064=1.0008\textrm{ s}\) at the measured sampling frequency.
This discrepancy is sufficiently small to be ignored, and throughout this tutorial we will assume a sampling frequency of \(sf=100\textrm{ Hz}\).

sf <- round(1/mean(si)) # mean sampling frequency (100 Hz)
dur <- 30               # duration of the processing window
y_cog <- head(          # 30 s of CoG Y samples
  df$y_cog[df$time>5],
  dur * sf
  ) 
x_cog <- head(          # 30 s of CoG X samples
  df$x_cog[df$time>5], 
  dur * sf
  ) 
time <- seq_len(dur * sf) / sf  # time vector (sf = 100 Hz)

We used the round() function to approximate the estimated sampling frequency to the nearest integer, which in this case is \(100\textrm{ Hz}\)5. We then used the head() function to retain \(3000\) samples (i.e., \(`dur * sf = 30 \times 100 = 3000\)) of both CoG Y and CoG X after the first \(5 \textrm{ s}\) of recording.
For convenience, the CoG coordinates in the two anatomical directions were stored in the variables y_cog and x_cog.




Demeaning Sway Data
Removing the mean from the data is not strictly necessary, but it simplifies both signal processing and visualization.
To demean \(y_\textrm{cog}\) and \(x_\textrm{cog}\), we simply subtract their respective mean values, computed using the mean() function:

y_cog = y_cog - mean(y_cog, na.rm = TRUE) # demeaned CoG Y
x_cog = x_cog - mean(x_cog, na.rm = TRUE) # demeaned CoG X

Notice the argument na.rm = TRUE passed to the mean() function. This ensures that missing values (NA) are ignored when computing the mean. Such values may arise, for example, if the final line of the imported csv file is empty. We can verify this by inspecting the last few observations with the tail() function:

tail(df,5) # the last 5 observations in df

         time     y_cog    x_cog
3993 39.95997 -78.58840 3.506513
3994 39.96998 -78.59058 3.503497
3995 39.97998 -78.58997 3.498837
3996 39.98999 -78.58673 3.494619
3997 40.00000        NA       NA






Plotting the Pre-Processed Data
The data should be visually inspected after each processing step to ensure that the intended operations have been correctly applied.
For example, the CoG displacements should remain within a few centimeters in both anatomical directions. In addition, after demeaning, both signals should be centered around \(0\textrm{ cm}\) and span the expected duration of \(30\textrm{ s}\).
Let us now overlay y_cog and x_cog to verify that these conditions have been met.


Show code
# first plot - y_cog stabilogram
plot(time, y_cog, 
  type = "l", 
  lwd = 8, 
  col = "lightcyan2", 
  xlab = "Time (s)", 
  main = "Stabilograms in the Sagittal and Frontal Planes",
  font.main = 2, 
  ylab = "CoG Position (cm)", 
  bty = "l"
  )

# second plot - x_cog stabilogram
lines(time, x_cog, 
  type = "l", 
  lwd = 2, 
  col = "turquoise4", 
  xlab = "Time (s)", 
  ylab = "CoG X Position (cm)", 
  bty = "l"
  )

# adding legend to distinguish the two traces
legend(
  "topleft", 
  inset = 0.02,
  c(expression("y"["cog"]),expression("x"["cog"])), 
  col = c("lightcyan2","turquoise4"), 
  lty = 1, lwd = c(8,2)
  )








Figure 7.7: Stabilograms with demeaned, CoG position data.








Computing CoG Metrics
As discussed in Chapter 2, numerous postural metrics have been proposed to characterize CoG trajectories [2,3]. In this tutorial, and throughout the course, we will focus on only three of them: the Elliptic Area (\(EA\)), the Standard Deviation (\(\sigma_{x,y}\)), and the Mean Speed (\(v_{x,y}\)).



Preliminaries on the Elliptic Area
In Section 2.2.5.2 (see Equation 2.12), we described the procedure used to compute the Elliptic Area. Briefly, we seek the two principal axes of the ellipse that best fits the CoG trajectory. These axes correspond to the directions along which the CoG coordinates are uncorrelated. Mathematically, this is achieved by finding the reference system that diagonalizes the CoG covariance matrix.
We begin by constructing the Statokinesigram6:


Show code
plot(x_cog, y_cog, 
  type = "l", 
  col = "turquoise4", 
  asp = 1,
  main = "Statokinesigram", 
  xlab = "Medial-Lateral (cm)", 
  ylab = "Anterior-Posterior (cm)"
  )

abline(h = 0, lty = "dotted") # adding HORIZONTAL line at 0 cm
abline(v = 0, lty = "dotted") # adding VERTICAL line at 0 cm








Figure 7.8: CoG coordinates projected on the surface.



The abline() function was used to draw horizontal and vertical reference lines. From Figure 7.8, we observe that, over the 30 s recording, the CoG trajectory spans approximately \(3\textrm{ cm}\) in the antero-posterior direction and \(3\textrm{ cm}\) in the medio-lateral direction. We also note that the principal excursion of the CoG follows a direction slightly rotated counterclockwise with respect to the vertical reference axis.
The axes of the ellipse that best fits the Statokinesigram (Figure 7.8) are computed according to Oliveira et al. [4] (see Equation 2.12).



The Covariance Matrix (\(\Sigma\))
We begin by computing the covariance matrix \(\Sigma\) from the CoG coordinates:

xy <- cbind(x_cog, y_cog) # matrix with N rows (sf * dur) and 2 cols
head(xy, 5)               # shows the first 5 rows of the xy matrix

         x_cog      y_cog
[1,] 0.5778717 -0.9954609
[2,] 0.6006753 -0.9810690
[3,] 0.6258725 -0.9732609
[4,] 0.6432517 -0.9697624
[5,] 0.6478979 -0.9688926

Sigma <- cov(xy)          # covariance matrix

We first used the cbind() function to combine the CoG \(x\) and \(y\) coordinates into a matrix with \(N=3000\) rows (equal to sf * dur) and two columns (x_cog and y_cog). We then computed the covariance matrix \(\Sigma\) using the cov() function from the stats package.
Inspecting Sigma, we observe that it is a symmetric \(2\times2\) matrix, as expected:

Sigma   # displays the covariance matrix

           x_cog      y_cog
x_cog  0.6404470 -0.5573457
y_cog -0.5573457  1.1368876


Its main diagonal contains the variances of \(x_\textrm{cog}\) and \(y_\textrm{cog}\), whereas the off-diagonal elements contain the covariance between the two coordinates. Because covariance is symmetric, these two elements are identical7:




The Ellipse Semi Axes
Our objective is to find a new covariance matrix \(\Lambda\) whose off-diagonal elements are both zero. This corresponds to identifying a new reference system (\(x',y'\)) in which the transformed coordinates are uncorrelated. We obtain this reference system by computing the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of \(\Sigma\) with the eigen() function:

xy_prime <- eigen(Sigma)  # computing eigenvalues and eigenvectors of Sigma 
xy_prime

eigen() decomposition
$values
[1] 1.4987882 0.2785463

$vectors
           [,1]       [,2]
[1,] -0.5445925 -0.8387008
[2,]  0.8387008 -0.5445925


xy_prime is a List containing two named elements: values and vectors. The eigenvalues are stored in xy_prime$values in decreasing order, while the corresponding eigenvectors are stored in xy_prime$vectors.






NoteWhat do these values and vectors represent?



The eigenvalues stored in xy_prime$values correspond to the variances of the CoG coordinates in the transformed reference system defined by \(x'\) and \(y'\). Consequently, the covariance matrix in this reference system is \(\Lambda=\mathrm{diag}(\lambda_1^2,\lambda_2^2)\).
The eigenvectors stored in xy_prime$vectors define the directions of the principal axes of this new reference system.


Their geometric interpretation becomes clearer when inspecting the figure below. For convenience, we store the eigenvalues and eigenvectors in separate R variables:


Show code
lambda <- sqrt(xy_prime$values) # square root of eigenvalues
eigvec <- xy_prime$vectors      # eigenvectors

# SEMI AXES of the ellipse, scalled with 1.96 standard deviation (1.96 lambda)
arrows(0, 0,                # origin of the vector 
       eigvec[1] * lambda[1] * 1.96, # x' coordinate of the first eigenvector
       eigvec[2] * lambda[1] * 1.96, # y' coordinate of the first eigenvector
       length = 0.05, 
       col = "pink", 
       lwd = 8
       ) # 








Figure 7.9: Semiaxes of the ellipse best fitting the CoG data.



Notice that we take the square root of the eigenvalues using the sqrt() function. This yields the standard deviations (\(\lambda_1\) and \(\lambda_2\)) of the CoG coordinates in the transformed reference system.
The ellipse semi-axes are shown using two different scaling factors: \(1.00\lambda\) and \(1.96\lambda\). Thus, the vectors displayed in Figure 7.9 have lengths equal to one and 1.96 standard deviations, respectively. The chosen scaling factor determines the size of the ellipse and, consequently, the proportion of CoG samples enclosed by it. For comparison, approximately 34% of observations project within \(\pm1.00\lambda\) along each principal axis, whereas approximately 47.5% project within \(\pm1.96\lambda\) along each axis individually.






NoteHow many CoG samples are included in the ellipse?



Using a scaling factor of \(1.96\), approximately 95% of the CoG samples project within the limits of each principal axis.
The resulting ellipse encloses approximately 85% of the CoG samples (see [4]).






Drawing the Ellipse
Drawing the ellipse is not necessary to compute the elliptic area, since this calculation only requires the eigenvalues (i.e., \(\Lambda=\mathrm{diag}(\lambda_1^2,\lambda_2^2)\); see Equation 2.12). However, overlaying the ellipse on the CoG trajectory is useful for verifying that the procedure has been implemented correctly: if so, the ellipse should provide a reasonable fit to the data.
We begin by creating an ellipse, scaling it according to the eigenvalues computed in the previous section, and plotting it:


Show code
plot(x_cog, y_cog, 
  type = "l", 
  col = "turquoise4", 
  asp = 1, 
  main = "CoG Statokinesigram", 
  xlab = "medial-lateral (cm)", 
  ylab = "Anterior-Posterior (cm)"
  )

abline(h = 0, lty = "dotted") # adding HORIZONTAL line at 0 cm
abline(v = 0, lty = "dotted") # adding VERTICAL line at 0 cm

x <- cos(
  seq(0, 2 * pi, length.out = 100)
  ) # x coordinates of the ellipse
y <- sin(
  seq(0, 2 * pi, length.out = 100)
  ) # y coordinates of the ellipse

x <- x * lambda[1] * 1.96 # scaling according to the first eigenvalue
y <- y * lambda[2] * 1.96 # scaling according to the second eigenvalue

lines(x, y, 
  type = "l", 
  col = "pink", 
  lwd = 8
  ) # drawing ellipse

legend(
  "bottomleft", 
  inset = 0.02, 
  c("CoG","Ellipse (1.96\U03BB)"),
  col = c("turquoise4","pink"), 
  lty = 1, 
  lwd = c(2, 8), 
  cex = 0.8
  )








Figure 7.10: Scaled ellipse in the \((x,y)\) reference system.



Although the ellipse appears to have been scaled correctly, it does not fit the data well. This occurs because the ellipse was drawn in the original \((x,y)\) reference system. We must therefore rotate it into the reference system defined by the principal axes, \((x',y')\). This can be done in R using the matrix multiplication operator (%*%):


Show code
xpyp <- cbind(x,y) %*% 
  t(eigvec)      # transforming between reference systems
xp <- xpyp[ , 1] # all elements of the first column of the transformed data
yp <- xpyp[ , 2] # all elements of the second column of the transformed data

# Then just rerun the commands above
plot(x_cog, y_cog, 
  type = "l", 
  col = "turquoise4", 
  asp = 1, 
  main = "CoG Statokinesigram", 
  xlab = "medial-lateral (cm)", 
  ylab = "Anterior-Posterior (cm)"
  )

abline(h = 0, lty = "dotted") # adding HORIZONTAL line at 0 cm
abline(v = 0, lty = "dotted") # adding VERTICAL line at 0 cm

lines(xp, yp, 
      type = "l", 
      col = "pink", 
      lwd = 8
      ) # drawing ellipse

legend(
  "bottomleft", 
  inset = 0.02, 
  c("CoG","Ellipse (1.96\U03BB)"), 
  col = c("turquoise4","pink"), 
  lty = 1, 
  lwd = c(2, 8), 
  cex = 0.8
  )








Figure 7.11: Scaled ellipse in the \((x',y')\) reference system.



We changed reference system by multiplying the \(x\) and \(y\) coordinates of the ellipse (cbind(x, y)) by the transpose (t()) of the eigvec matrix, which contains the two eigenvectors, one in each column.





Computing the CoG Elliptic Area
After identifying the axes of the ellipse that best fits the CoG data (see Preliminaries on the Elliptic Area), we compute the elliptic area \(EA\) using Equation 2.12, restated here:
\[
   EA = \pi k^2 \sqrt{\lambda_1 \lambda_2}
\]
where \(\sqrt{\lambda_1}\) and \(\sqrt{\lambda_2}\) are the standard deviations of the CoG coordinates in the reference system aligned with the ellipse semi-axes (see Figure 7.11), and \(k\) is the scaling factor determining the proportion of data points included in the ellipse. For \(k=1.96\), the ellipse encloses approximately 85% of the CoG samples.
For the data shown in Figure 7.11, the CoG elliptic area is EA = \(7.8 \textrm{ cm}^2\):

EA = pi * 1.96^2 * prod(lambda)
EA

[1] 7.797958






Mean Speed and Standard Deviation
Computing these stabilometric metrics simply requires translating the equations introduced in Section 2.2.5.1 into code. They are restated below for convenience.
Several procedures could be used to compute these two metrics. Both require processing all \(N=3000\) CoG samples, corresponding to the \(30\textrm{ s}\) processing window defined when Trimming Sway Data. Since loops in R will only be introduced in Chapter 8, we will use a different approach here.






NoteDo it yourself



Unlike the Elliptic Area, no code will be provided for computing these two metrics. This task is left as an exercise at the end of this chapter.





Mean CoG Speed (\(v_{x_\textrm{cog}}\) and \(v_{y_\textrm{cog}}\))
We may either:

compute the total CoG displacement and divide it by the duration of the processing epoch, \(\frac{N-1}{sf}=29.99\textrm{ s}\);
compute the \(N-1=2999\) instantaneous speed values from each pair of consecutive CoG samples and then average them.

Both approaches lead to the following equation:
\[
  v_x=\frac{sf}{(N-1)}\sum_{i = 1}^{N-1} |x_\textrm{cog}(i+1)-x_\textrm{cog}(i)|
\]
where \(x_\textrm{cog}\) denotes the CoG coordinate in the frontal plane8 (medio-lateral direction), sampled at frequency \(sf\) for a total of \(N\) samples.




CoG Standard Deviation (\(\sigma_{x_\textrm{cog}}\) and \(\sigma_{y_\textrm{cog}}\))
The CoG Standard Deviation is defined as:
\[
  \sigma_x=\sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i = 1}^{N} \left(x_\textrm{cog}(i)-\bar{x}_\textrm{cog}\right)^2}
\]
where \(x_\textrm{cog}\) and \(\bar{x}_\textrm{cog}\) denote, respectively, the CoG coordinate and the mean CoG position in the frontal plane9 (medio-lateral direction).
Notice that \(\bar{x}_\textrm{cog}=0\) because of the Demeaning Sway Data procedure described above. Therefore, in this case, the Standard Deviation and Root Mean Square values coincide.






Note\(N\) or \(N-1\)?



Some authors use a slightly different definition of standard deviation, with \(N-1\) instead of \(N\) in the denominator.
The reason for this distinction will become clear later in the course. Because the number of CoG samples is typically large, the two definitions can be considered practically equivalent.

title: 7  Plotting and Processing Raw Data
text:
7.4 Exercises
Compute the Mean CoG Speed (\(v_{x_\textrm{cog}}\) and \(v_{y_\textrm{cog}}\)) and the CoG Standard Deviation (\(\sigma_{x_\textrm{cog}}\) and \(\sigma_{y_\textrm{cog}}\)) for the two csv files available in the course material, separately for the medio-lateral (\(x\)) and antero-posterior (\(y\)) directions.

Remember to do this after trimming and demeaning the data.
The R sd() function is not allowed for computing \(\sigma_{y_\textrm{cog}}\).
The R functions and operators suggested for this task are diff(), sum(), mean(), ^, and %*%.

To check whether your computations are correct, the results for each of the two files are listed in Table 7.1.





Table 7.1: CoG standard deviation and speed for the two files provided.















[1] Carpenter MG, Frank JS, Winter DA, Peysar GW. Sampling duration effects on centre of pressure summary measures. Gait & Posture 2001;13:35–40. https://doi.org/https://doi.org/10.1016/S0966-6362(00)00093-X.


[2] Jacono M, Casadio M, Morasso PG, Sanguineti V. The sway-density curve and the underlying postural stabilization process. Motor Control 2004;8:292–311. https://doi.org/10.1123/mcj.8.3.292.


[3] Prieto TE, Myklebust JB, Hoffmann RG, Lovett EG, Myklebust BM. Measures of postural steadiness: Differences between healthy young and elderly adults. IEEE Transactions on Biomedical Engineering 1996;43:956–66. https://doi.org/10.1109/10.532130.


[4] Oliveira LF, Simpson DM, Nadal J. Calculation of area of stabilometric signals using principal component analysis. Physiological Measurement 1996;17:305–12. https://doi.org/10.1088/0967-3334/17/4/008.
